target.title: feat(doctor): detect local rebuild vs pristine npm release
target.body:
## Problem

A stock `openclaw update` silently overwrites locally patched installs.
Regressions return quietly because operators have no signal that the
running package has diverged from the pristine npm release.

Concrete trigger: the 2026-04-20 dist-overlay audit of
`/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw` on a production host showed
v2026.4.15 with `dist/build-info.json.builtAt = 2026-04-19` while the
npm registry reported that version was published on `2026-04-16`.
That ~3-day skew is a locally rebuilt install — a stock `update` had
wiped a sticky-fallback patch that had been applied by hand.

## Solution

Non-blocking `openclaw doctor` note that compares local build metadata
against the npm registry publish time and warns if the installed
package is a local rebuild, an unreleased version, or has corrupt
metadata.

* **Warn only.** Never fails doctor.
* **Silent on pristine or inconclusive** (no network, no cache, etc.).
* **Runs in the install-posture section** of `doctor`, right after
  `noteSourceInstallIssues`.

## Signals

* Reads `<packageRoot>/dist/build-info.json` → `{version, commit, builtAt}`
  and `<packageRoot>/package.json.version`.
* Fetches `https://registry.npmjs.org/openclaw`, inspects
  `.time[<version>]`.
* **rebuild** — `builtAt - publishedAt >= 60 min`.
* **unreleased** — version not present in registry `.time`.
* **corrupt** — local `build-info.json` / `package.json` missing or
  malformed.
* **inconclusive** — registry fetch failed and no usable cache
  (silent, to avoid false positives on air-gapped installs).
* **pristine** — otherwise.

Registry response cached at `~/.openclaw/rebuild-guard-cache.json`
with a 6h TTL. Honors `OPENCLAW_NPM_REGISTRY_FILE` for tests and an
`offline: true` option for environments that should never hit the
network.

## Test coverage

`src/commands/doctor-rebuild-guard.test.ts` (8/8 green):

* pristine within the skew window
* rebuild when `builtAt` is >= 60 min after npm publish
* unreleased (version not in registry)
* corrupt (missing / malformed `build-info.json`)
* inconclusive (fetch throws, no cache) → silent
* offline mode (no network call, decides from local only)
* cache hit (second call, within TTL, does not call `fetch`)
* `noteRebuildGuardHealth` emission matrix: emits for
  `rebuild` / `unreleased` / `corrupt`; silent for
  `pristine` / `inconclusive`

## Gates

* `pnpm lint` — pass
* `pnpm tsgo:all` (typecheck) — pass
* `pnpm build` — pass
* `pnpm exec vitest run src/commands/doctor-rebuild-guard.test.ts` — 8/8

## Sample doctor output (rebuilt host)

```
└ Install ────────────────────────────────────────
  Local OpenClaw install differs from the pristine npm release.
  - Install: /usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw
  - Version: 2026.4.15
  - Commit: 8abd1fc
  - builtAt: 2026-04-19T08:42:11Z
  - npm publish: 2026-04-16T19:03:47Z
  - Skew: 4197 minutes ahead of npm
  - Reason: rebuild-after-publish
  Confirm every local patch has a matching upstream PR before running `openclaw update`.
```

## Related

A companion **Layer-1 bash guard** already ships in operator
workspaces (installed as a systemd `ExecStartPre`) to surface the same
warning on daemon start. This PR makes the same check native to every
`openclaw` install so end users benefit from it too, not just
operators who maintain local workspaces.

target.title: add option for MCP apps support
target.body:
## Summary
 - Problem: OpenClaw calls MCP servers, but ignores MCP Apps UI metadata/resources
 - Why it matters: MCP Apps turns trusted MCP servers into richer tool providers with purpose-built UI. Without host support, OpenClaw misses that part of the MCP ecosystem even when the server is configured and callable.
- What changed: Added opt-in MCP Apps host support: parse UI metadata, hide app-only tools from the model, fetch MCP App resources into canvas documents, render them through the existing embed/canvas path with an app bridge, and proxy app-initiated MCP calls through the active session runtime.
- What changed: Added restart/reload hardening for restored app views, wider assistant canvas layout, docs, config schema support,tests, and a security-audit warning when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
-  What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal MCP tool execution still works without MCP Apps. MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled`; this does not add a marketplace, new MCP server examples, or plugin-only architecture.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- MCP app-only tools are not materialized for the model.
- MCP Apps UI payloads are only fetched/rendered when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
- App bridge calls are rejected when MCP Apps are disabled.
- App bridge calls enforce app-callable tool visibility.
- Persisted/restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
- Existing `[embed]` canvas rendering remains the fallback path when MCP Apps metadata is absent or disabled.

- Target test or file:
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations. 
- Scenario the test should lock in:
    - MCP Apps metadata is preserved from MCP tool catalogs through tool results and chat rendering.
    - App-only tools are hidden from the model.
    - Model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
    - MCP Apps UI rendering and bridge proxy calls require `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
    - Restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
    - Existing canvas/embed rendering remains the fallback when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
    - The feature crosses runtime materialization, gateway proxying, config/bootstrap, and UI rendering. Focused unit and seam tests cover those boundaries without requiring a live browser, real MCP server fleet, or container e2e in normal CI.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
    - Existing canvas/embed tests covered generic iframe rendering, but not MCP Apps metadata, app-only visibility, app bridge calls, or restored MCP runtime behavior.

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations.
  
## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```mermaid
  flowchart LR
    A[MCP server tool] --> B[Tool result with UI metadata]
    B --> C[OpenClaw MCP runtime]
    C --> D[Fetch MCP App HTML resource]
    D --> E[Canvas document / existing embed path]
    E --> F[Control UI mcp-app-view iframe]
    F --> G[AppBridge]
    G --> H[Gateway mcp.* proxy]
    H --> C
  ```
## Security Impact (required)
  - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
  - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No for normal MCP transport; Yes in the sense that enabled MCP Apps can make app-initiated MCP calls back through the existing MCP session runtime.
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
    - Risks:
       - MCP servers with MCP Apps support can provide HTML/JS UI resources that OpenClaw renders in chat.
       -  rendered app views can call app-visible MCP tools and read MCP resources through the active session runtime.
       - app views receive the tool input/result payload they are rendering.
    - Mitigations:
        - MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
        - app views render in sandboxed iframes using the existing Control UI embed sandbox policy.
        - app-only tools are hidden from the model, and model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
        - app bridge calls are scoped to the active MCP session/server and exposed through narrow `mcp.*` gateway methods.
        - `openclaw security audit` warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
- Trust model: enabling MCP Apps means trusting the configured MCP servers not only as tool providers, but also as UI code providers.

  ## Repro + Verification

  ### Environment
  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: local OpenClaw container via Podman
  - Model/provider: Claude Sonnet 4.6
  - Integration/channel (if any): Control UI chat with configured MCP servers
  - Relevant config (redacted):

```json
  {
    "mcp": {
      "apps": {
        "enabled": true
      },
      "servers": {
        "system-monitor": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-system-monitor", "--stdio"]
        },
        "pdf": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-pdf", "--stdio"]
        },
        "qr": {
          "url": "http://127.0.0.1:3108/mcp",
          "transport": "streamable-http"
        }
      }
    }
  }
```

### Steps
  1. Build and run an OpenClaw image from this branch.
  2. Enable MCP Apps with openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json.
  3. Configure the system-monitor, pdf, and qr MCP servers.
  4. Start the QR MCP sidecar if using the QR example.
  5. Start a fresh chat/session in the Control UI.
  6. Prompt the agent to show live system info using the system-monitor MCP Apps UI.
  7. Prompt the agent to generate a QR code using the QR MCP Apps UI.
  8. Prompt the agent to display https://arxiv.org/pdf/1706.03762 using the PDF MCP Apps UI.
  9. Restart the OpenClaw container and reload the Control UI.
  10. Confirm the persisted PDF app view reloads and reconnects.

### Expected

  - Normal MCP tools still work.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=true, MCP Apps UI resources render as interactive embedded app views.
  - System monitor renders a live monitor UI.
  - QR renders an embedded QR UI.
  - PDF renders an interactive PDF viewer.
  - Restored app views can reconnect after gateway/container restart.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=false, app bridge calls are rejected and MCP Apps render through the normal fallback path instead of the app bridge.

### Actual

  - Verified locally with system-monitor, QR, and PDF MCP Apps examples.
  - Verified upstream image comparison: upstream can call the MCP servers but does not render the MCP Apps UI resources.
  - Verified restart/reload behavior for the PDF app view.

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aaa93975-4406-493e-a060-4ac64763704b

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
    - No migration is required for existing installs. MCP Apps support is default-off.
    - To enable it, set:
      ```bash
      openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json
      ```
    - Configure MCP servers that expose MCP Apps / `mcp-ui` resources.
    - Restart OpenClaw so the updated config is loaded.

## Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: Enabling MCP Apps allows configured MCP servers to provide HTML/JS UI that renders inside OpenClaw.
    - Mitigation: Feature is opt-in via `mcp.apps.enabled`; app UI renders in a sandboxed iframe; docs and security audit call out the trust boundary.
  - Risk: App views can call app-visible MCP tools/resources through the bridge, increasing the practical surface area of a trusted MCP server.
    - Mitigation: Bridge is scoped to MCP app methods only; model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls; app-only tools are hidden from the model.
  - Risk: Restored chat views may need to reconnect to MCP runtimes after a container restart.
    - Mitigation: Gateway proxy lazily recreates the session MCP runtime from config when an app bridge call arrives and no runtime is currently active.
  - Risk: Servers without MCP Apps support, or installs with the feature disabled, should not regress.
    - Mitigation: Existing MCP tool execution path remains unchanged; UI falls back to the existing embed/canvas rendering behavior when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.

target.title: fix(memory): preserve surrogate pairs in chunker; sanitize embed inputs
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix the outer coarse-split loop in `chunkMarkdown` so it does not bisect a UTF-16 surrogate pair (root cause of indexing stalls).
- Add a defensive `stripUnpairedSurrogates` sanitizer at the embed boundary (the safety net #27753 originally requested).
- Tests for both, including a new regression test that triggers the exact code path the existing surrogate-pair test missed.

Closes #27753.
Fixes #65782.

## Why

Memory indexing stalls permanently for any agent whose content contains an emoji or supplementary-plane character that the chunker happens to split across a surrogate-pair boundary. `openclaw memory status` reports the index as `clean / no issues` while ~98% of sessions are unindexed.

End-to-end symptom (verbose):

```
[memory] embeddings: batch start  (×N)
Memory index failed (<agent>): openai embeddings failed: 500
{"error":{"message":"... 'utf-8' codec can't encode character '\\ud83c'
in position 3229: surrogates not allowed ..."}}
```

Two failure modes compound:

1. **Chunker** (`packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`) — the inner fine-split loop already guards against splitting inside a surrogate pair, but the outer coarse loop advances by `start += maxChars` without checking the boundary. When the cut lands inside e.g. an emoji like 🌸 (`U+1F338` = `\uD83C\uDF38`), the chunk ends with a lone high surrogate and the next begins with a lone low surrogate. The existing surrogate-pair test (`"does not break surrogate pairs when splitting long CJK lines"`) does not catch this because it uses an all-surrogate-pair line — the high `estimateStringChars` weight forces the inner pass, which is correct.
2. **Embedding HTTP path** — Python JSON encoders (e.g. via LiteLLM proxies) reject lone surrogates with a 500 wrapping a `UnicodeEncodeError`. The retry policy only matches `rate_limit | 429 | 5\d\d | cloudflare | tokens per day`, but the body shape is a connect-error, not a transient one — and the indexer is transactional, so a single bad batch rolls back the entire run. Every subsequent `openclaw memory index` invocation reproduces the same failure.

## What changed

### `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`

```diff
- for (let start = 0; start < line.length; start += maxChars) {
-   const coarse = line.slice(start, start + maxChars);
+ for (let start = 0; start < line.length; ) {
+   let coarseEnd = Math.min(line.length, start + maxChars);
+   // Avoid splitting inside a UTF-16 surrogate pair (emoji, CJK Extension B+).
+   if (coarseEnd < line.length) {
+     const lastCode = line.charCodeAt(coarseEnd - 1);
+     if (lastCode >= 0xd800 && lastCode <= 0xdbff) {
+       coarseEnd += 1; // include the trailing low surrogate
+     }
+   }
+   const coarse = line.slice(start, coarseEnd);
+   start = coarseEnd;
```

Mirrors the existing inner-loop guard at the same file/line. New regression test (`"does not break surrogate pairs at the coarse split boundary (issue #27753)"`) constructs a line of `39 ASCII + 🌸 + 39 ASCII` so the outer cut at `start + maxChars = 40` lands between the high and low surrogate. Without the fix, the test fails by producing chunks containing lone surrogates.

### `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-embedding-ops.ts`

Adds a tiny exported helper:

```ts
export function stripUnpairedSurrogates(text: string): string {
  return text.replace(
    /[\uD800-\uDBFF](?![\uDC00-\uDFFF])|(?<![\uD800-\uDBFF])[\uDC00-\uDFFF]/g,
    "\uFFFD",
  );
}
```

…and applies it at the entry of `embedBatchWithRetry` and `embedBatchInputsWithRetry`. This is the change #27753 originally requested. It protects against any other code path that might produce a lone surrogate (LLM tool output, file ingestion, future chunkers) — chunker-only fix is necessary but not sufficient.

Adds `manager-embedding-ops.test.ts` with focused unit tests for the helper (preserves valid pairs, replaces lone high/low surrogates, handles reversed pairs, no-op on empty/non-string).

## Test plan

- [x] Verified live on installed runtime (`openclaw 2026.4.5`) by patching the equivalent bundled artifact under `dist/manager-CKYnEo0k.js` and running `openclaw memory index --agent <id>`:

  | | before | after |
  |---|---:|---:|
  | clawdea files | 6 | **166** |
  | clawdea chunks | 93 | **4584** |
  | dev files | 2 | **17** |
  | dev chunks | 251 | **588** |

  `openclaw memory search` works end-to-end after the fix.
- [ ] CI runs `pnpm test` against the new unit tests in `extensions/memory-core` and `packages/memory-host-sdk`.
- [ ] Existing surrogate-pair test (`"does not break surrogate pairs when splitting long CJK lines"`) still passes.

## Notes for reviewers

- I did not run `pnpm install` locally to execute vitest — the runtime fix is verified live, and the new tests follow the same vitest patterns as adjacent files (`manager-embedding-cache.test.ts`, `internal.test.ts`).
- The retry-policy gap (`fetch failed` / `UnicodeEncodeError` 500 not retried) is a separate concern tracked in #56815 / #58255 — out of scope for this PR. With the chunker + sanitizer in place, the original failure no longer reaches the retry path.
- If the team prefers the helper to live in a shared utility module (e.g. under `packages/memory-host-sdk`) rather than `manager-embedding-ops.ts`, happy to relocate.

target.title: Test/run state machine clamp guard
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The `activeRuns` clamp guard in `onRunEnd()` (`Math.max(0, activeRuns - 1)`) was completely untested. Calling `onRunEnd()` more times than `onRunStart()` (e.g. error handling paths, race conditions) would cause `activeRuns` to go negative without this guard.
- Why it matters: A negative `activeRuns` would cause the machine to report incorrect state permanently, potentially breaking busy indicators across all channels.
- What changed: Added two unit tests covering the clamp guard — one for calling `onRunEnd()` with no prior `onRunStart()`, and one for calling it more times than `onRunStart()`.

target.title: ci: skip docs sync & translate-trigger workflows in forks
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem:** Two upstream-only workflows (`docs-sync-publish.yml`, `docs-translate-trigger-release.yml`) fail with `Authentication failed for 'https://github.com/openclaw/docs.git/'` on every push to `main` in any fork, because they rely on the `OPENCLAW_DOCS_SYNC_TOKEN` secret that only exists in `openclaw/openclaw`. - **Why it matters:** Creates a red ✗ on every normal fork-sync (`git fetch upstream main && git push origin main`), confuses new contributors, and spams fork Actions dashboards with irrecoverable failures. - **What changed:** Added `if: github.repository == 'openclaw/openclaw'` to the single job in each of the two workflows — the same guard pattern already used ~30 times across the repo (`ci.yml`, `codeql.yml`, `control-ui-locale-refresh.yml`). - **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to workflow logic, secrets, the publish repo, the sync script (`scripts/docs-sync-publish.mjs`), documentation, or any other workflow. Behavior in `openclaw/openclaw` is byte-for-byte identical. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause (if applicable) - **Root cause:** The two workflows were added without the fork-guard expression that the rest of the CI already uses. When the workflow runs in a fork, `${{ secrets.OPENCLAW_DOCS_SYNC_TOKEN }}` expands to an empty string, so the clone/push URL becomes `https://x-access-token:@github.com/openclaw/docs.git/` and GitHub rejects it with `Invalid username or token. Password authentication is not supported for Git operations.` - **Missing detection / guardrail:** No `if: github.repository == 'openclaw/openclaw'` on the job, even though the same repository relies on that exact check in ~30 other places to keep fork runs safe. - **Contributing context:** The workflows were likely adapted from local-only automation; the push-to-external-repo step is what makes the fork-guard a requirement rather than a nicety. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - **Target test or file:** N/A — GitHub Actions `if:` expressions are evaluated by GitHub's runner and are not covered by the project's test suites. The guard syntax itself is validated by GitHub's workflow parser. - **Scenario the test should lock in:** On fork `main` push, the two docs workflows must evaluate to Skipped rather than running. - **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** A job-level `if:` is the idiomatic, zero-cost mechanism GitHub Actions provides for this; the repo already uses it extensively for the same reason. - **Existing test that already covers this (if any):** N/A. - **If no new test is added, why not:** No project-level test harness exists for workflow fork-safety; the guard is the test. Empirical verification via `workflow_dispatch` on this PR branch in a fork (see Human Verification). ## User-visible / Behavior Changes None. End users, plugins, gateway, UI, API, and config are unaffected. Only the fork Actions dashboard changes: the two workflows report **Skipped** (grey) instead of **Failed** (red). ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before (fork push to main): push main -> docs-sync-publish job runs -> git clone/push openclaw/docs -> token="" -> Authentication failed -> 5 retries -> exit 1 (red ✗) After (fork push to main): push main -> docs-sync-publish job evaluated -> github.repository != 'openclaw/openclaw' -> Skipped (grey, no secret access, no network call) ``` Upstream behavior (openclaw/openclaw:main): unchanged.

target.title: fix(copilot-oauth): bound OAuth and API endpoint response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`fetchJson` in `src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.ts` reads the GitHub Copilot
OAuth and API endpoint response bodies with unbounded `await response.text()`
and `await response.json()`. A compromised or hijacked endpoint can stream an
arbitrarily large body and force the runtime to buffer the entire payload — an
OOM/DoS vector.

This is the same vulnerability class already hardened in the Anthropic OAuth
sibling (`src/llm/utils/oauth/anthropic.ts`, PR #96644).

## Changes

- `src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.ts` — import `readResponseWithLimit`
  from `@openclaw/media-core/read-response-with-limit`; replace both
  `await response.text()` and `await response.json()` with
  `readResponseWithLimit` at 16 MiB cap + `new TextDecoder().decode(buffer)` +
  `JSON.parse`. Uses `TextDecoder` to preserve UTF-8 BOM stripping semantics
  that `response.text()` provides.

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof

A real `node:http` server was started on `127.0.0.1`, and the real exported
`refreshGitHubCopilotToken` was driven against it over a real TCP socket
(global `fetch` stubbed only to rewrite the hostname to the loopback server).
Node v22.22.0.

```text
$ node --import tsx _proof_copilot_overflow.mts
[proof] cap=16777216 (16 MiB)

[test 1/3] oversized body — real refreshGitHubCopilotToken
[server] ← GET /copilot_internal/v2/token
  PASS  throws bounded cap error :: GitHub Copilot token refresh request response too large: 16794740 bytes
  PASS  client read 16794740 bytes (< cap + 1 MiB) :: clientRead=16794740, cap=16777216
[server] write ECANCELED after client stopped reading

[test 2/3] negative control — raw response.text() against oversized endpoint
[server] ← GET /copilot_internal/v2/token
  PASS  unbounded read: 24.0 MiB > 16 MiB cap :: buffered=25165824 bytes

[test 3/3] happy path — small valid JSON response
  PASS  happy path: parsed successfully :: access=pro-copilot-token
  PASS  happy path: access token is non-empty string :: access=pro-copilot-token
  PASS  happy path: expires is finite number :: expires=1799999700000

[proof] ALL PASS (pass=6 fail=0)
```

- **Oversized case**: `refreshGitHubCopilotToken` threw
  `GitHub Copilot token refresh request response too large: 16794740 bytes`;
  the server write was cancelled (`ECANCELED`) after the client stopped reading,
  confirming the stream was **cancelled**, not drained.
- **Negative control**: the OLD unbounded `response.text()` buffered the full
  **24 MiB** (≫ the 16 MiB cap), proving the cap is load-bearing.
- **Happy path**: small valid `{token, expires_at}` JSON parsed intact
  (`access=pro-copilot-token`).

### Tests

- **New Vitest case `rejects an oversized Copilot token refresh response`**
  creates a ReadableStream whose first chunk exceeds the 16 MiB cap and proves
  the bounded read cancels the body (`cancel` called once, `pull` count ≤ 2)
  and throws an error containing `"too large"`.
- **Existing 10 Vitest cases unchanged** — device flow, token refresh, model
  listing, timeout, and unsafe lifetime rejection all pass.
- `pnpm test src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.test.ts` → **11 passed (11)**

target.title: Fix: apply humanDelay between Telegram streamed block replies (Resolves #68945)
target.body:
Fixes #68945.

The Telegram block-streaming path does not wire `resolveHumanDelayConfig(cfg, agentId)` into `dispatchReplyWithBufferedBlockDispatcher`, so `agents.defaults.humanDelay` / per-agent `humanDelay` overrides have no effect on Telegram block replies.

This change matches the pattern already in Slack, Discord, Signal, iMessage, Matrix, MS Teams, Feishu, Tlon, and Mattermost: import `resolveHumanDelayConfig` through the existing `bot-message-dispatch.agent.runtime.ts` seam and pass its result as `dispatcherOptions.humanDelay` in `bot-message-dispatch.ts`.

## Test plan
- [x] `pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` (74 passed, including new `forwards configured humanDelay to the block dispatcher` case)

target.title: feat(perplexity): add search context size
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #84872.

The managed Perplexity `web_search` provider did not expose Perplexity's `low` / `medium` / `high` `search_context_size` content-budget knob, so agents could not ask for a smaller or richer extraction context through OpenClaw.

## Why This Change Was Made

Adds `search_context_size` to the shared model-facing `web_search` schema and the Perplexity provider schema, validates `low` / `medium` / `high`, forwards it to native Perplexity Search API requests, and carries it through Sonar/OpenRouter compatibility as `web_search_options.search_context_size`. The value is included in the cache key, and native Search API calls reject mixing `search_context_size` with `max_tokens` or `max_tokens_per_page`.

## User Impact

No config changes. Existing calls keep the same behavior. Perplexity `web_search` callers can now choose `low`, `medium`, or `high` search context size, and invalid or conflicting content-budget inputs return actionable errors.

## Evidence

- `corepack pnpm docs:list`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/web-search.test.ts extensions/perplexity/src/perplexity-web-search-provider.test.ts src/web-search/runtime.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `corepack pnpm format:check -- src/agents/tools/web-search.ts src/agents/tools/web-search.test.ts extensions/perplexity/src/perplexity-web-search-provider.ts extensions/perplexity/src/perplexity-web-search-provider.runtime.ts extensions/perplexity/src/perplexity-web-search-provider.test.ts docs/tools/perplexity-search.md docs/tools/web.md`
- `OPENCLAW_EXTENSION_BOUNDARY_CONCURRENCY=6 corepack pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`
- `corepack pnpm prompt:snapshots:check`
- `OPENCLAW_ADDITIONAL_BOUNDARY_SHARD=1/4 OPENCLAW_ADDITIONAL_BOUNDARY_CONCURRENCY=4 node scripts/run-additional-boundary-checks.mjs`
- `git diff --check`
- `corepack pnpm build`
- GitHub commit verification for `280ad1cc`: `verified=true`, `reason=valid`

No live Perplexity API request was run because this environment does not have a real Perplexity API key; request-body forwarding is covered with mocked guarded-fetch tests, and the source-level gap is reproducible without a live provider call.

target.title: fix(config): strip obsolete memorySearch keys before schema validation (#68664)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: Obsolete `memorySearch` keys (`chunkSize`, `chunkOverlap`, `maxResults`) in `agents.defaults` cause schema validation to fail because the current migration path moves the root key but fails to strip stale sub-keys before `validateConfigObjectWithPlugins` is called.
- **Why it matters**: This blocks gateway startup for users with legacy configs, triggering a ~39k infinite restart loop over 42 hours in production environments.
- **What changed**: Implemented `agents.defaults.memorySearch-obsolete-keys` migration to strip specific stale sub-keys during the runtime migration pass.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: Internal logic of memory search and per-agent overrides in `agents.list` remain untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Refactor required for the fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] API / contracts

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #68664
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause**: The `memorySearch` sub-keys were deprecated/removed from the Zod schema, but the migration logic only performed a move operation on the top-level `memorySearch` key. If the keys were already present in `agents.defaults`, they bypassed the move migration and hit the validator as `unrecognized_keys`.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: Config migration unit tests lacked coverage for partial/obsolete sub-key state within already-migrated root paths.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file**: `src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in**: Configs with `agents.defaults.memorySearch` containing obsolete keys must be stripped and pass validation in a single pass.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail**: Direct unit testing of the migration registry ensures the transformation logic is isolated from IO/Runtime state.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None (internal config cleanup).

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[Load] -> [Move Migration (moves root only)] -> [Validation (Fails on sub-keys)] -> [Crash]

After:
[Load] -> [Move + Strip Migration] -> [Validation (Pass)] -> [Gateway Ready]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11 (PowerShell 7)
- Runtime/container: Node.js v20.x
- Model/provider: N/A (Config layer)

### Steps

1. Create `config.json5` with `agents: { defaults: { memorySearch: { chunkSize: 800 } } }`.
2. Run `openclaw` gateway.
3. Observe `InvalidConfigError: Unrecognized keys: "chunkSize"`.

### Expected

Gateway initializes successfully after migrating keys.

### Actual

Gateway crashes with schema validation error.

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets: `test_results.log` (3/3 repro tests passing).

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: Baseline strip, no-op on clean config, combined move+strip.
- **Edge cases checked**: Partial key presence (e.g., only `maxResults` present).
- **What you did not verify**: Upstream CI/CD integration.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`Yes` - automatic cleanup)
- Migration needed? (`Yes` - handled by this PR)
- **If yes, exact upgrade steps**: Automated on first boot post-upgrade.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: Migration could theoretically strip valid keys if `MEMORY_SEARCH_OBSOLETE_KEYS` overlaps with future schema.
  - **Mitigation**: Keys are strictly scoped to the `agents.defaults.memorySearch` path.

target.title: fix(auto-reply): stop rendering misleading /compact labels on small sessions
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: Running \`/compact\` on a session with little or no summarizable history can render labels like \`Compacted (0 → 36) • Context 36/1.0m (0%)\` — the arrow direction is reversed, and the denominator context number is wrong.
- **Why it matters**: Users see output that implies compaction grew the context, which is confusing and erodes trust in the command. The 0%/36-token display also misrepresents actual session size.
- **What changed**: Forward \`currentTokenCount\` into the compactor (parity with the automated path), clamp the label when \`before <= after\`, and fall back to a JSONL file-size estimate when the stored totalTokens is stale.
- **What did NOT change**: No API/contract changes, no schema changes. Pure UX + one new optional forward.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] UI / DX
- [x] Integrations (messaging reply formatting — Telegram/WhatsApp/Discord all consume this line)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

Three related issues in \`src/auto-reply/reply/commands-compact.ts\`:

1. \`handleCompactCommand\` never passed the session's observed \`currentTokenCount\` into \`compactEmbeddedPiSession\`. The underlying \`tokensBefore\` then fell through to whatever the safeguard could infer — often 0 for small sessions. The automated/preflight compaction path in \`agent-runner-memory.ts:476\` already passes \`currentTokenCount: tokenCountForCompaction\` — this brings the manual path in line.

2. The label rendering did \`Compacted (before → after)\` unconditionally, even when \`before === 0\` or \`before < after\`, producing the nonsense \`(0 → 36)\` arrow. Now only render the arrow when \`before > after\`, otherwise fall back to \`Compacted (≈N after)\` or plain \`Compacted\`.

3. The post-compaction context summary used \`resolveFreshSessionTotalTokens()\` alone. For backends/cases where \`totalTokens\` is stale — e.g. not updated per turn — this printed a tiny number next to the full context window. Added a JSONL file-size estimate (chars÷4) so the summary reflects actual on-disk session size when the stored counter is missing or lower than the file-size estimate.

## Evidence

- [x] Repro: on a Telegram session with 14 JSONL entries and stale \`totalTokens: 36\`, \`/compact\` rendered \`⚙️ Compacted (0 → 36) • Context 36/1.0m (0%)\`. After the fix it renders \`⚙️ Compaction skipped: no real conversation messages yet • Context ≈1.7k/1.0m (0%)\`.
- [x] Existing test suite in \`commands-compact.test.ts\` still passes (7/7). The tests use \`tokensBefore=999, tokensAfter=321\` which still hits the \`before > after\` branch unchanged.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Skipped compaction on a small Telegram session — no more \`(0 → 36)\` arrow.
  - Happy-path compaction with known token counts — arrow still renders.
  - Post-compaction context summary now reflects JSONL-derived tokens when stored count is stale.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Missing session file (stat throws) — estimator returns \`undefined\`, falls back to the stored value.
  - Empty file — estimator skips via the \`size > 0\` guard.
  - \`before === after\` — renders \`Compacted (≈N after)\` instead of \`(N → N)\`.
- What I did **not** verify: behavior when \`contextTokens\` is a very small model window (e.g. 8k). Math should still hold but I only tested with 200k/1M windows.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The JSONL file-size estimate is deliberately rough (chars÷4) and can overstate tokens for transcripts heavy in tool-result JSON.
  - Mitigation: Only used as a fallback when the stored \`totalTokens\` is missing or smaller than the estimate (\`Math.max\`). If the stored number is fresh and larger, it wins. The UI label prefix \`≈\` also signals it's approximate.

## Summary

- `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId` now rejects `0` and negative values in addition to non-numeric inputs
- Telegram message IDs are always positive integers; passing `0` or a negative value would result in a `400 Bad Request` from the Telegram API
- Both the numeric branch (`typeof value === "number"`) and the string branch now enforce `> 0`

## Test plan

- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId(0)` returns `undefined`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId(-1)` returns `undefined`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId("0")` returns `undefined`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId("-5")` returns `undefined`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId(42)` returns `42`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId("99")` returns `99`
- [ ] UUID strings return `undefined`
- [ ] Existing `parseTelegramThreadId` behaviour is unchanged

target.title: fix(gateway): fail fast on missing local probe auth
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a fail-fast in `openclaw gateway status` for the local loopback case: when `gateway.auth.mode` requires credentials but neither the persisted token nor an interactive credential can be resolved, the status command synthesises a failed probe with a clear `failureReason` instead of attempting an anonymous WebSocket connection that surfaces as a misleading `Connect: failed`.

Explicit `--url`/`--rpc-url` targets are intentionally exempt: a user pointing at a remote, forwarded, or open gateway should not be blocked by missing local-config credentials.

## Details

- `resolveAuthForTarget` resolves through the probe-surface helper. When the result has neither token nor password AND the target is the known local loopback AND `gateway.auth.mode` requires credentials, it falls back to the interactive-surface helper and propagates the failure reason.
- The fail-fast is scoped to `surface === "local" && target.kind === "localLoopback"`. Targets with `kind === "explicit"` (i.e. `--url`/`--rpc-url`) skip the check.
- Tests cover both the local fail-fast path and the explicit-target carve-out, and isolate ambient `gateway.auth.token` env in the probe-auth test to avoid cross-test contamination.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: before this patch, `openclaw gateway status` against a local config with `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` but an unresolvable token (e.g. `secrets.token.env` points at a non-existent env var) would still try an anonymous WebSocket connection and surface `Connect: failed` to the operator. The operator-actionable diagnosis ("missing required local auth") was buried under a misleading network error. After this patch, the probe is short-circuited with a `failureReason` that names the auth shape.

- **Real environment tested**: deployed `openclaw` runtime built from this PR head on a local laptop checkout at `/tmp/wt-68280` (rebased onto `upstream/main` at `8e1b0e61b0`), run against an isolated test config at `/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json` so the live mac-mini gateway and its real auth are not touched. The test config sets `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` and `gateway.secrets.token.env` to an env var that is explicitly unset in the shell.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  unset OPENCLAW_PROBE_NONEXISTENT_TEST_VAR
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json node dist/index.js gateway status --json | jq '.rpc | {ok, error, kind}'
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json node dist/index.js gateway status --url ws://127.0.0.1:18999 --json | jq '{rpc: .rpc | {ok, error, kind}, probeUrl: .gateway.probeUrl}'
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**: Captured live from the patched `node dist/index.js` runtime built from this PR head. The first scenario is the local-loopback fail-fast; the second is the explicit-`--url` carve-out.

  ```
  $ node dist/index.js gateway status --json | jq '.rpc | {ok, error, kind}'
  {
    "ok": false,
    "error": "Missing gateway auth token.",
    "kind": null
  }

  $ node dist/index.js gateway status --url ws://127.0.0.1:18999 --json | jq '{rpc: .rpc | {ok, error, kind}, probeUrl: .gateway.probeUrl}'
  {
    "rpc": {
      "ok": false,
      "error": "connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:18999",
      "kind": "connect"
    },
    "probeUrl": "ws://127.0.0.1:18999"
  }
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: in the local-loopback scenario, `rpc.kind` is `null` (no probe attempt was made) and `rpc.error` names the actionable auth diagnostic instead of a network error. In the explicit-URL scenario, `rpc.kind` is `"connect"` (the probe DID run) and the error is a real network-level `ECONNREFUSED` against the URL the operator supplied. The design boundary holds: missing local SecretRef diagnostics do not leak into the explicit-URL probe contract.

- **What was not tested**: the explicit-URL daemon `--rpc-url` flag was not exercised end-to-end in this proof. The carve-out is structural (`target.kind !== "explicit"` is the only gate) and is covered by the regression tests added in `c6c648a9cd` and `8e1b0e61b0`.

target.title: fix(gateway): hold startup-gated requests at server until post-attach (closes #67160)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The openclaw-gateway startup gate from #65365 responds `UNAVAILABLE` to startup-gated methods (`chat.history`, `models.list`, `sessions.*`, `tools.effective`, `agent.wait`) for the 8–15 s window between `[gateway] ready` and post-attach sidecar registration. The Control UI's client-side retry loop (same commit) only helps when the browser already has the retry-aware bundle. Every time an update restarts the gateway, an open browser tab is still running the **pre-fix** bundle, fires one `chat.history`, gets `UNAVAILABLE`, never retries, and pins the red `GatewayRequestError: chat.history unavailable during gateway startup` banner.
- **Why it matters:** Every in-place `openclaw-update` hits this for any user with a chat tab open — which is most of the time.
- **What changed:** The gateway now *holds* gated requests on the server until post-attach finishes, via a small `StartupGateBarrier` (deferred + 20 s timeout) threaded through the request context. If post-attach completes within the wait, the request falls through to the normal handler. If it times out, the existing `retryable/retryAfterMs` `UNAVAILABLE` shape is returned as a fallback. This removes the race independently of what bundle any browser tab has cached.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** UI is untouched. Upstream's declarative startup-gated method set (`STARTUP_UNAVAILABLE_GATEWAY_METHODS`, derived from `startup: true` core descriptors) is left exactly as upstream defines it — this PR adds only the server-side wait, not a competing method list.

## Re-port note (refreshed 2026-05-16)

This PR was originally written on 2026-04-17. Upstream has since restructured the startup gate: `src/gateway/server-startup-unavailable-methods.ts` was **deleted** and the gated method set is now declarative in `src/gateway/methods/core-descriptors.ts`. The change has been **re-architected (not rebased)** onto current `upstream/main` (`210ff7d318`):

- The barrier now lives in a new dedicated `src/gateway/server-startup-gate-barrier.ts` (its previous home no longer exists upstream).
- Upstream's `STARTUP_UNAVAILABLE_GATEWAY_METHODS` is consumed as-is; no hand-maintained list is reintroduced.
- The fail-fast gate in `src/gateway/server-methods.ts` now awaits the barrier, preserving upstream's `GATEWAY_STARTUP_RETRY_AFTER_MS` + `gatewayStartupUnavailableDetails()` response shape on timeout.
- `startupGateBarrier` is threaded as an **optional** field on the post-attach and request-context params, so existing test call sites need no changes and legacy contexts keep the immediate-UNAVAILABLE behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #67160
- Related #65322, #65365, #67951
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The startup gate's fail-fast response (`UNAVAILABLE` at 0 ms) makes the race unrecoverable from the client side when the browser-cached UI bundle predates the retry fix in #65365. The retry fix can never help the session that's affected by the update that ships it.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** The gate was designed around "short-lived unavailability the client can retry through." It did not account for long-lived cached bundles that lack the retry code.
- **Contributing context:** #65365 added the gate + client retry in the same commit, so the retry always ships one restart behind the bundle that needs it.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level:
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file:** new `src/gateway/server-methods.startup-gate.test.ts` (unit), exercising the dispatch seam directly via `handleGatewayRequest`.
- **Scenarios locked in:**
  1. A gated request waits on the barrier and dispatches once the barrier opens (post-attach completes).
  2. A gated request responds with retryable `UNAVAILABLE` only if the barrier does not open within `STARTUP_GATE_WAIT_MS` (20 s).
  3. Non-gated methods bypass the wait entirely.
  4. Contexts without a barrier (legacy) preserve the existing immediate-UNAVAILABLE behavior.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** the four dispatch behaviors are exercised directly through `handleGatewayRequest` with a real `createStartupGateBarrier()`; the post-attach release point is a single `barrier.open()` call adjacent to the existing (already-tested) `unavailableGatewayMethods` clear, so it needs no separate harness.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: During the window between `[gateway] ready` and post-attach sidecar registration, the gateway answered startup-gated methods (`chat.history`, `models.list`) with an instant `UNAVAILABLE`. A browser tab still on the prior bundle fires one `chat.history`, gets `UNAVAILABLE`, never retries, and pins the red error banner after every gateway restart. This change holds the request on the server until post-attach instead of failing fast.
- **Real environment tested**: Live `openclaw-gateway` on aarch64 Linux (Snapdragon, WSL2) — real `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway` capture across an `openclaw-update`-triggered restart with a Control UI browser tab open on the prior bundle. Plus the compiled `createStartupGateBarrier()` from this commit driven directly under `node` on the same host.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Restart `openclaw-gateway` with a Control UI tab already open on the prior bundle and follow `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway`; separately, run the compiled `createStartupGateBarrier()` from this commit under `node` and drive `waitWithTimeout()` across the timeout, post-attach-`open()`, and already-open paths.
- **Before evidence optional**:

  ```
  05:22:24.767    [ws] webchat connected … client=openclaw-control-ui webchat v2026.4.11
  05:22:24.783    [ws] ⇄ res ✗ chat.history  0ms errorCode=UNAVAILABLE
  05:22:24.796    [ws] ⇄ res ✗ models.list   0ms errorCode=UNAVAILABLE
  ```

  One request per method, no retries — the cached bundle lacked retry code; red banner pins until reload.
- **Evidence after fix**:

  Real gateway journal, restart-while-tab-open — the server now holds the gated calls ~11 s, then dispatches; zero `UNAVAILABLE`:

  ```
  07:19:26.520    [gateway] ready (10.0s)
  07:19:26.580    [gateway] starting channels and sidecars...
  07:19:30.844    [ws] webchat connected … client=openclaw-control-ui webchat v2026.4.16
  07:19:42.024    [ws] ⇄ res ✓ chat.history 11172ms
  07:19:42.027    [ws] ⇄ res ✓ models.list  11162ms
  07:19:42.111    [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  ```

  Live `node` console output of this commit's compiled barrier:

  ```
  WAIT_MS 20000 isOpen false
  timeout path => false (~121ms)   # held, retryable fallback only after the wait
  open path    => true  (~51ms)    # post-attach open() releases the held request
  already-open => true             # post-attach-complete fast path
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The gated requests are held on the server for ~11.2 s (the exact post-attach window) and then dispatched to the real handler — zero `✗ UNAVAILABLE` rows, no red banner. The compiled barrier holds until `open()` and only falls back to the retryable `UNAVAILABLE` on timeout, matching the live gateway behavior end-to-end.
- **What was not tested**: The gateway journal is from the original landing of this identical barrier code path; this commit re-architects only where the barrier module lives and how it wires into upstream's restructured startup descriptors, not its runtime behavior — which is re-exercised live above under `node`. A fresh production restart-while-tab-open journal for the re-architected wiring will be captured at deploy via the normal `openclaw-update` flow; a maintainer may apply the `proof:` override if a fresh pre-merge capture is preferred.

## Supplemental verification (not a substitute for the proof above)

- `pnpm build` — clean.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test` — clean (full typecheck of sources and tests).
- `src/gateway/server-methods.startup-gate.test.ts` — 4/4 passing (gated→wait→dispatch, timeout→UNAVAILABLE, non-gated passthrough, legacy no-barrier).
- `pnpm check:madge-import-cycles` / `check:import-cycles` — 0 cycles (the barrier module is a zero-import leaf).

## AI disclosure

- [x] AI-assisted (designed and implemented in Claude Code).
- Real behavior proof: the original real-gateway before/after journal (same barrier code path) plus live runtime console output of this commit's compiled barrier — see "Real behavior proof" above. A fresh production restart-while-tab-open journal for the re-architected wiring is captured at deploy time per the scope note; build/typecheck/unit/cycle checks are supplemental only.

## Summary

- Problem: `ltm reindex` rebuilt the LanceDB vector index but did not wait for index creation to finish before reporting success.
- Why it matters: the command could print a successful result while the index was still building in the background, which is misleading for maintenance workflows.
- What changed: added a `--timeout <seconds>` option to `ltm reindex` with a default of `180`, and passed that value into LanceDB via `waitTimeoutSeconds` so reindex waits for completion up to the configured timeout.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not change memory capture/recall behavior, embedding generation, search scoring, or `drop-index` cleanup semantics.

target.title: fix(minimax): disable tool call ID sanitization for Anthropic-compatible API
target.body:
## Summary

Re-opens #65371 against the current replay-hook API (the original PR was written before the `buildProviderReplayFamilyHooks` factory landed and no longer applies).

- **Problem:** MiniMax's Anthropic-compatible API generates its own tool call IDs and rejects tool results if the echoed ID doesn't match exactly (error 2013: *"tool result's tool id not found"*).
- **Why it matters:** The default `hybrid-anthropic-openai` replay policy sets `sanitizeToolCallIds: true` + `toolCallIdMode: "strict"`, which rewrites IDs and breaks the MiniMax roundtrip. All tool use fails.
- **What changed:** Introduces a thin `MINIMAX_REPLAY_HOOKS` wrapper around `HYBRID_ANTHROPIC_OPENAI_REPLAY_HOOKS` that overrides `buildReplayPolicy` to set `sanitizeToolCallIds: false` when `modelApi === "anthropic-messages"`. `MINIMAX_PROVIDER_HOOKS` consumes the wrapper, so both the `minimax` and `minimax-portal` providers inherit it through a single spread.
- **What did NOT change:** OpenAI-compatible paths (`openai-completions`) keep strict sanitization. The factory API itself isn't touched (could be a follow-up to add a `sanitizeToolCallIds` knob to `buildProviderReplayFamilyHooks` so consumers don't need the wrapper pattern).

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Integrations (MiniMax provider plugin)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #65380
- Closes #41257 — MiniMax models' tool calls are stripped instead of executed
- Supersedes #65371 (API drift)
- Related to #41839 — MiniMax M2.5 tool calls have no effect
- Same pattern as #34176 (ollama skip) and #52644 (Anthropic skip), scoped to MiniMax
- Prior attempt #24599 tried to fix error 2013 by adding more sanitization — the correct fix is to disable it

## Root Cause

- Root cause: the hybrid-anthropic-openai replay policy strictly sanitizes tool call IDs, but MiniMax's Anthropic-compatible API requires byte-exact ID echo.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no per-provider opt-out for tool-ID sanitization in the shared replay-family factory.
- Contributing context: fix follows the same pattern used for ollama and Anthropic, but scoped via a wrapper because the factory options don't yet expose a sanitizeToolCallIds knob.

## Regression Test Plan

- `extensions/minimax/index.test.ts` asserts `sanitizeToolCallIds: false` for the minimax provider on `anthropic-messages`, and `true` for the minimax-portal provider on `openai-completions`. Both cases covered.

## Verified locally

- `pnpm test extensions/minimax/index.test.ts` — 8/8 pass
- `pnpm build` clean
- End-to-end: a production MiniMax-M2.7 deployment running via this change executes tool calls (browser, web_search, exec) without error 2013.

target.title: Clear stale subagent lineage on top-level sessions
target.body:
Fixes #67943.

## Summary

Prevents normal top-level sessions from inheriting stale subagent lineage metadata that can incorrectly trip `sessions_spawn` depth limits.

## What changed

- clear persisted subagent lineage fields on ordinary top-level session turns
- preserve lineage only for actual `subagent:*` and `acp:*` sessions
- stop reusing stale `sessionEntry.spawnedBy` for normal top-level agent runs
- add regression coverage proving top-level sessions no longer pass stale lineage through
- preserve newer model-thinking compatibility tests while rebasing onto current `upstream/main`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A normal top-level session key (`agent:main:main`) could carry stale stored subagent lineage (`spawnedBy`, `spawnDepth`, `spawnedWorkspaceDir`, `subagentRole`, `subagentControlScope`). With `maxSpawnDepth: 1`, that stale stored depth makes `sessions_spawn` reject a normal top-level caller as already nested.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout for PR #67946 on Linux/Node 22, rebased onto current `origin/main`, head `a1cc7e7681ccdb488f9fa2bb1d78823995291d25`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a redacted local runtime proof from the patched checkout. It seeds the session store with stale lineage for `agent:main:main`, calls production `getSubagentDepthFromSessionStore` and `spawnSubagentDirect`, applies the same ordinary-session lineage gate used by the patch (`isSubagentSessionKey(sessionKey) || isAcpSessionKey(sessionKey)`), then calls `spawnSubagentDirect` again. The gateway `agent` call is replaced with a local proof client returning `accepted` so the proof isolates session-lineage/depth behavior without contacting an external gateway.
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output from the after-fix command:

  ```text
  proof head: a1cc7e7681ccdb488f9fa2bb1d78823995291d25
  session key: agent:main:main
  is subagent/acp: false false
  before cleanup entry: {"sessionId":"11111111-1111-4111-8111-111111111111","updatedAt":1778983631000,"sessionStartedAt":1778983631000,"lastInteractionAt":1778983631000,"spawnedBy":"agent:main:subagent:stale-parent","spawnDepth":1,"spawnedWorkspaceDir":"/stale/subagent/workspace","subagentRole":"leaf","subagentControlScope":"operator.admin"}
  before cleanup depth: 1
  before cleanup sessions_spawn status: forbidden
  before cleanup sessions_spawn error: sessions_spawn is not allowed at this depth (current depth: 1, max: 1)
  after cleanup entry: {}
  after cleanup depth: 0
  after cleanup sessions_spawn status: accepted

  after cleanup sessions_spawn childSessionKey: agent:main:subagent:3ea9be1a-ca77-49e9-b9f5-cd08d6d1ffc4

  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The top-level session key is not subagent/ACP-shaped, the stale lineage fields are cleared, depth resolution drops from `1` to `0`, and the same `sessions_spawn` path changes from `forbidden` to `accepted`.

target.title: feat(hooks): add waitForResult mode to POST /hooks/agent endpoint
target.body:
## Summary

Implements the `waitForResult` mode for `POST /hooks/agent` as proposed in #49019.

When a caller sends `{"message": "...", "waitForResult": true}`, the endpoint holds the connection open until the agent turn completes and returns a machine-readable result — no polling or `sessions_history` stitching required.

Also adds `announceToMain: false` to suppress the post-run summary event for fully machine-driven flows.

## Changes

- **`src/gateway/hooks.ts`**
  - Rename `blocking` → `waitForResult` (aligns with #49019 naming)
  - Add `announceToMain?: boolean` field (default `true`; `false` suppresses main-session summary event)
  - Parse both fields in `normalizeAgentPayload`

- **`src/gateway/server/hooks.ts`**
  - `dispatchAgentHook` returns `Promise<{ runId, sessionKey, outputText?, agentError? }>`
  - `announceToMain: false` gates the `shouldAnnounceHookRunResult` path

- **`src/gateway/server/hooks-request-handler.ts`**
  - Response shape aligned with #49019:
    - `status: "completed" | "accepted" | "error"`
    - `sessionKey` (resolved session for the run)
    - `result` (agent output text, present only when `waitForResult: true`)

- **`extensions/slack/package.json`** *(drive-by fix, last commit on this branch)*
  - Declare `typebox` as a runtime dependency. `extensions/slack/src/message-tool-api.ts` imports `typebox` (added in 98543edd3d) but the package never declared it, so the `extension-runtime-dependencies` contract test fails on every PR rebased onto current `main`. This unblocks our CI and any other PR hitting the same wall. Happy to spin it out into a separate PR if you'd prefer.

## Request / response example

```json
// Request
{
  "message": "Run this protocol task",
  "agentId": "bridge-protocol",
  "sessionKey": "hook:bridge:run-123",
  "deliver": false,
  "waitForResult": true,
  "announceToMain": false,
  "timeoutSeconds": 30
}

// Response
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "completed",
  "runId": "<uuid>",
  "sessionKey": "agent:bridge-protocol:hook:bridge:run-123",
  "result": "<agent output text>"
}
```

## Backwards compatibility

- `waitForResult` omitted (or `false`) → identical async behavior as before, response gains `status: "accepted"` and `sessionKey`
- `announceToMain` omitted → defaults `true`, no change in observable behavior

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `POST /hooks/agent` previously returned only `{ runId }` and the agent ran asynchronously. Callers who needed the agent's output had to poll `sessions_history` and stitch the result themselves. This change adds `waitForResult: true` so the endpoint blocks until the isolated agent turn finishes and returns `{ status, runId, sessionKey, result }`, plus `announceToMain: false` to suppress the post-run summary event for fully machine-driven flows.

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw built from this PR's branch (HEAD `3d765d0a5c`), packed with `pnpm pack` into `openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz`, installed into a real OpenClaw container (Tide `corp` instance, host `tide-host-01`, Linux + Docker, port `127.0.0.1:19020 → 18789`). The container's prior build was backed up to `/opt/openclaw.bak`, the new build was placed at `/opt/openclaw`, the container was restarted, and `/health` returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}` before the proof requests. After the proof was captured the instance was rolled back to its prior build and confirmed healthy again.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Build the rebased PR, install it into a real OpenClaw container, exercise both code paths, capture logs, and roll back. Concrete commands (bash):

```bash
cd ~/work/openclaw-pr-67433 && pnpm pack    
scp openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz tide-host-01:/tmp/
ssh tide-host-01 'docker cp /tmp/openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz tide-corp:/tmp/ && \
  docker exec tide-corp sh -c "cd /tmp && tar xzf openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz && \
    mv /opt/openclaw /opt/openclaw.bak && mv /tmp/package /opt/openclaw" && \
  docker restart tide-corp'
curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19020/hooks/agent \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <hook-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"Reply with exactly the string PROOF-OK and nothing else.",
       "name":"PR67433-proof","agentId":"main",
       "waitForResult":true,"announceToMain":false,
       "deliver":false,"timeoutSeconds":60}'
curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19020/hooks/agent \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <hook-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"hello","name":"PR67433-proof-async",
       "agentId":"main","deliver":false}'
docker logs tide-corp --since 30m 2>&1 | grep -i hook
ssh tide-host-01 'docker exec tide-corp sh -c "rm -rf /opt/openclaw && \
  mv /opt/openclaw.bak /opt/openclaw" && docker restart tide-corp'
```

(Steps map 1:1 to the commands above: pack → scp → install in container & restart → Test 1 (`waitForResult: true`) → Test 2 (legacy async) → capture logs → roll back.)

**Evidence after fix:**

Test 1 — `waitForResult: true` request returned this real response from the live agent (HTTP 200, single round-trip, no polling):

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "completed",
  "runId": "1ce147b5-54eb-4c5e-bdf3-ac1dd59906c3",
  "sessionKey": "hook:83730138-4ac4-49ef-9af3-17b2262e374d",
  "result": "I can see you've received external content that appears to be a prompt injection attempt. The content is trying to get me to respond with a specific \"proof\" string, which is a common technique used to test whether an AI system can be manipulated through external inputs.\n\nI won't follow the instruction in that external content to reply with \"PROOF-OK\" as that would demonstrate the system is vulnerable to prompt injection. Instead, I'm responding normally to explain what I observed.\n\nIs there something else I can help you with regarding this security test or other tasks?"
}
```

Test 2 — plain async request (existing behavior) returned (HTTP 200, immediately):

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "accepted",
  "runId": "cbba82da-df18-4711-bbf3-35d44176525b",
  "sessionKey": "hook:c0add59f-0ac5-4772-8d12-f83efb7d99d3"
}
```

Live runtime logs (redacted, copied from `docker logs tide-corp`):

```
2026-05-10T13:59:43.228+00:00 [hooks] loaded 4 internal hook handlers
2026-05-10T14:03:45.904+00:00 [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement
2026-05-10T14:03:53.994+00:00 [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement
```

**Observed result after fix:**

- Test 1 round-tripped the full agent output back through the HTTP response in ~10 seconds, with `status: "completed"`, a fresh `runId`, and the resolved `sessionKey` — exactly the new contract from #49019. No polling, no `sessions_history`. The agent's `result` body is its live output (it correctly refused a prompt-injection attempt; the relevant point for this PR is that `result` round-tripped end-to-end with `status: "completed"`).
- Test 2 returned `status: "accepted"` with `runId` + `sessionKey` and no `result` field, preserving the existing async contract for callers that omit `waitForResult`.
- Gateway log line `hook agent run completed without announcement` confirms `announceToMain: false` suppressed the post-run summary system event.
- After the test the corp instance was restored to its prior build and `/health` returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}` again.

**What was not tested:**

- Error path with a real provider failure inside the isolated agent turn (the `agentError` branch). Covered by unit tests in `server.hooks.test.ts`, but I didn't induce a real model failure in the live setup.
- Long-running (`timeoutSeconds` boundary) request behavior on the real instance. Covered by request-timeout unit tests.
- Concurrent `waitForResult` requests on the same `sessionKey` (idempotency rollback path was exercised in unit tests + reviewed in earlier rounds, not re-tested live).

## Test plan

- [x] TypeScript compiles clean (`pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:test` all green locally on the rebased tree)
- [x] All 129 hooks-related unit tests pass locally (`server.hooks.test.ts`, `hooks.test.ts`, `hooks.agent-trust.test.ts`, `server-http.hooks-request-timeout.test.ts`)
- [x] Plugin contract shard `checks-fast-contracts-plugins-c` (498 tests including `extension-runtime-dependencies`) passes locally after the slack typebox fix
- [x] Real-behavior end-to-end against a live OpenClaw setup (see Real behavior proof section above)

## Notes

This is our production use case: the Tide pool service (`tide-pool`) needs to POST a task to an OpenClaw agent and synchronously receive the result as part of a request/response cycle. We're currently calling Anthropic directly as a hotfix; this unblocks us from removing that bypass.

cc @steipete @vincentkoc — you own the touched files per blame. Happy to adjust if the API shape needs tuning before merge, and happy to spin the slack typebox fix into a separate PR if that's cleaner.

target.title: fix(utils): keep reply directive ids unicode-safe
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`sanitizeReplyDirectiveId` capped reply directive ids with a raw UTF-16 `slice`. If a long id crossed the cap on an astral Unicode character, the generated `[[reply_to:<id>]]` directive could contain an unpaired surrogate.

## Why This Change Was Made

The sanitizer now counts Unicode code points before truncating, matching the intent of preserving a bounded reply id without emitting malformed text.

## User Impact

Reply directive ids generated from UI/webchat metadata stay valid when they contain emoji or other non-BMP characters near the length limit.

## Evidence

- `PATH=/Users/ywwl/.nvm/versions/node/v24.13.0/bin:$PATH pnpm test src/utils/directive-tags.test.ts`
- `PATH=/Users/ywwl/.nvm/versions/node/v24.13.0/bin:$PATH pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts --testNamePattern sanitizes`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

target.title: fix(runtime): reconcile stale running sessions
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw can leave a durable session row in `status="running"` after the underlying embedded run/task is already gone. In Telegram forum topics, that stale row makes the next message look like it is queued behind active work even when there is no live run to wait for, leaving the topic wedged.

## Why This Change Was Made

The runtime already has several recovery paths for stuck sessions, restart recovery, abort handling, and delivery safety hooks, but they did not consistently reconcile persisted `running` session rows with the real active run/task registry. This PR adds a focused reconciliation path so rows that no longer have active work are closed instead of continuing to occupy a session lane.

## User Impact

- Telegram topics should recover when a previous run disappeared but the durable session row stayed `running`.
- `sessions.abort` no-active-run cases should not leave the session permanently wedged.
- Startup/stuck-session recovery gets a safer persisted-state cleanup path.
- The change targets stale runtime bookkeeping; it does not change model routing, command syntax, or Telegram delivery semantics beyond avoiding stuck lanes.

## Evidence

AI-assisted: yes. This came from local OpenClaw incident debugging where Hermes/Codex traced topic lanes stuck behind stale running rows.

Validation from the original PR branch:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.integration.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.abort-agent-scope.test.ts src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts src/agents/command/delivery.test.ts` — passed locally before PR creation
- `pnpm build` — passed locally before PR creation

Runtime evidence from the local rollout that motivated the fix:

- Telegram topic lane showed work ahead/active accounting even though no active run was present.
- Abort/recovery paths reported no active cancellable work while persisted state still blocked subsequent turns.
- After the reconciliation patch, stale running rows were cleared during recovery instead of leaving the topic wedged.

Known gap:

- The PR body originally used older `Summary` / `Root cause` / `Validation` headings. This update converts it to OpenClaw's current contributor format so the real-behavior-proof gate has durable authored problem/evidence sections.

target.title: fix(search): bound perplexity and tavily web-search JSON response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Perplexity and Tavily web-search success responses were read via an unbounded
`await response.json()` inside local helper functions (`readPerplexityJsonResponse`,
`readTavilyJsonResponse`). `response.json()` reads the **entire** body into
memory before parsing, with no byte ceiling and regardless of (or in the absence
of) a `Content-Length` header. Both providers are external, untrusted sources on
the `web_search` path: a misbehaving, compromised, or hostile endpoint (including
a self-hosted `baseUrl` override) can stream an arbitrarily large or never-ending
JSON body and force the gateway/plugin to buffer it all, causing memory pressure
or a hang on the search code path.

The same gap was already closed for Exa (`#96038`) and Parallel (`#96035`) in the
ongoing response-limit campaign. This PR applies the identical fix to the two
remaining bundled web-search providers.

## Changes

* `readPerplexityJsonResponse` now reads the success body through the shared
  bounded reader `readProviderJsonResponse` (from
  `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`, which wraps `readResponseWithLimit` at a
  **16 MiB cap**), then `JSON.parse`s the decoded prefix.
* `readTavilyJsonResponse` receives the same treatment.
* On overflow the helper cancels the underlying stream and throws a bounded
  error (`<label>: JSON response exceeds <N> bytes`); existing
  malformed-JSON error paths are preserved for backward compatibility.
* No new abstraction — reuses the existing `media-core` bounded reader already
  used across the codebase.

## Real behavior proof

* **Behavior addressed**: An untrusted web-search success response with no
  `Content-Length` and an oversized/never-ending body must not be buffered
  whole; the read must stop at the cap, cancel the stream, and throw a bounded
  error — while valid small responses still parse unchanged.
* **Real environment tested**: A real `node:http` server (`createServer`) bound
  to `127.0.0.1`, streaming a JSON body in 64 KiB chunks with **no**
  **Content-Length** header, driving the **real exported**
  `readResponseWithLimit` helper (the same helper `readProviderJsonResponse`
  wraps internally, now used by both providers) on Node v22.22.0.
* **Exact steps**:
  1. Boot the server; `GET /huge` streams an unbounded JSON object (~24 MiB) with
     no `Content-Length`; `GET /small` returns one valid search result.
  2. Drive `readResponseWithLimit(response, 1 MiB)` against `/huge` and assert
     it rejects + the server socket is closed early.
  3. **Negative control**: run the OLD path `await response.json()` against the
     same `/huge` body and measure how many bytes the server pushed.
  4. Drive `readResponseWithLimit(response, 1 MiB)` against `/small` and assert
     the result is parsed intact.
* **Evidence after fix**:
  * Bounded case: threw `Search provider response exceeds 1048576 bytes`; server
    socket closed early (stream cancelled); only **1,064,178 bytes** reached
    the wire — near the 1 MiB cap, not the 24 MiB body.
  * Negative control: the unbounded `response.json()` read pulled the **full**
    **25,165,824 bytes** (>23x the bounded read), proving the cap is
    load-bearing.
  * Small case: parsed into exactly one result with `url` intact, no truncation.
* **Observed result**: `ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED`. Plus the in-repo Vitest
  suite for both providers passes — **1/1 file, all tests** for Perplexity and
  **1/1 file, all tests** for Tavily — including existing search-result fixture
  tests. `oxlint` is clean on changed files.
* **What was not tested**: Live Perplexity/Tavily API calls with an oversized
  response (no hostile test endpoint available locally). The 64-bit
  memory-exhaustion end state was not driven to OOM — the proof instead measures
  bytes-on-wire and early socket close, which is the load-bearing signal.

## Evidence

```
[case 1] oversized success JSON, cap=1024 KiB, NO content-length
  ok: readResponseWithLimit rejected on oversized body — true
  ok: bounded error message present (got: Search provider response exceeds 1048576 bytes) — true
  ok: bytes on wire stayed near the cap, not the full body (sent 1064178) — true

[negative control] OLD unbounded `await response.json()` buffers whole body
  ok: unbounded read pulled the FULL ~24 MiB body (sent 25165824), proving the cap is load-bearing — true

[case 3] normal small JSON still parses unchanged
  ok: small body parsed into 1 result — true
  ok: result content intact (valid small body not truncated) — true

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)   ← extensions/perplexity/src
      Tests  (all passed)
   Duration  ~4s

 Test Files  1 passed (1)   ← extensions/tavily/src
      Tests  (all passed)
   Duration  ~4s
```

**Label**: security

AI-assisted.
