target.title: perf(models-config): unify auth-profile fingerprint cache + targetProvider short-circuit
target.body:
## Summary

Improves `models.json` preparation so OpenClaw can skip expensive implicit-provider discovery when the relevant inputs are unchanged, while still re-planning when canonical auth state, plugin catalogs, or generated config inputs drift.

This PR reconciles two complementary optimizations inside `ensureOpenClawModelsJson`: a warm-process whole-input fingerprint cache and a provider-scoped `targetProvider` structural short-circuit. Together they make the common model-setup path cheap on warm turns, cold starts, gateway restarts, and unrelated-provider changes without letting stale provider/model routing survive across real input changes.

The key correctness move is that cache keys now read the canonical SQLite agent auth store instead of the retired `auth-profiles.json` file. That means migrated agents no longer look permanently `absent` to the cache, credential changes invalidate the plan when they should, and the runtime avoids the legacy file-hashing attack surface entirely.

## Why

`ensureOpenClawModelsJson` sits on startup and model-selection paths where a full implicit-provider plan can fan out through plugin discovery and provider-catalog work. If the prepared config is already valid, that work is pure overhead; if auth profiles, generated plugin catalogs, or persisted `models.json` inputs changed, reusing the old plan is a routing-integrity bug.

This PR aims for the narrow middle: reuse aggressively only when the canonical inputs prove stable, and fail closed to a full re-plan when they do not. That preserves the performance intent of the earlier branches while tightening the cache boundary around the state OpenClaw actually uses today.

## What Changed

- Adds an in-process whole-input fingerprint cache for warm `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` calls.
- Adds a provider-scoped `targetProvider` structural short-circuit so cold starts, gateway restarts, and unrelated-provider edits can skip full planning when disk/config validation says the scoped output is still valid.
- Fingerprints the canonical SQLite auth store (`openclaw-agent.sqlite`, via the warm pooled handle) instead of legacy `auth-profiles.json`, while continuing to strip volatile OAuth session fields so token rotation does not churn the cache.
- Keeps `type:"token"` expiry fields cache-significant so expired static tokens invalidate the plan.
- Threads generated plugin catalogs into the pre-plan parsed `models.json` and writes them consistently through skip, noop, and write paths.
- Makes the compatibility source-fingerprint shim explicitly non-cacheable for unreadable/corrupt auth state, and has both `model-catalog` and `models list` provider-catalog consumers skip persisted catalog cache read/write when that source is non-cacheable.
- Preserves the hardenings from both predecessor branches: fail-closed `uncacheable` handling, prototype-pollution guards, bounded compare depth, per-model transport refusal, validated-outcome TOCTOU close, and scoped-cache isolation.

## Relationship to Prior PRs

Supersedes #73260 and #73261 with one reconciled implementation. They were complementary, not redundant:

- **#73260** added content-hash auth-profile fingerprinting plus `models.json` drift detection. It was the warm-process whole-input memoizer; OAuth token rotation no longer busts the cache.
- **#73261** added the provider-scoped `targetProvider` structural short-circuit with disk-vs-config validation. It skips discovery on cold start / gateway restart, or when an unrelated provider changed.

Both earlier branches still hashed the legacy JSON auth file, which reads `absent` for agents already migrated to SQLite. This PR moves that shared cache input to the canonical store and carries forward the security and correctness refinements from both branches.

## Verification

Exact-head local verification on `e5671e9b27`:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/models-config.fingerprint-cache.test.ts src/agents/model-catalog.test.ts src/commands/models/list.provider-catalog.test.ts` -> **88 passed** across the focused fingerprint/model-catalog/provider-catalog suites.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` -> clean.
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test` -> clean.
- `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-90741-plugin-catalog-cache-validation.ts` -> all 5 runtime assertions passed.
- `git diff --check` -> clean.

## Real behavior proof

Real-runtime proof script `scripts/proof-90741-plugin-catalog-cache-validation.ts` drives the real `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` path against a real on-disk agent directory, real generated plugin-catalog sidecars, the real `readyCache` populate/drift cycle, the real no-create auth-store read, and the public `buildModelsJsonSourceFingerprint` shim. **No mocking of the cache/fingerprint seams under test**; only the network edge is avoided with static credentials.

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Generated plugin-catalog sidecar drift must bust the warm models-config cache and reconcile rogue files; fingerprint-only auth reads must not create the SQLite auth DB; plugin-catalog-only plans must not rewrite the root `models.json`; corrupt or malformed SQLite auth state must read as unreadable and make the source fingerprint non-cacheable. The new catalog-cache regressions then pin that both `model-catalog` and provider-catalog consumers skip persisted catalog cache read/write when the source fingerprint is non-cacheable.
- **Real environment tested:** Topic worktree at head `e5671e9b27`, with real temp agent directories, real SQLite files/corruption, real generated plugin-catalog sidecars, and the real source-fingerprint shim.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-90741-plugin-catalog-cache-validation.ts`, run from the topic worktree.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured stdout from the live run:

```
[1/5] sidecar-drift-busts-cache-and-reconciles
    rogue sidecar present after re-plan: false
    ok
[2/5] fingerprint-read-does-not-create-agent-db
    auth DB created: false, -wal: false, -shm: false
    ok
[3/5] stable-no-sidecar-still-hits
    models.json mtimeMs cold=1781500389439.0088 warm=1781500389439.0088 ino cold=4067810 warm=4067810
    ok
[4/5] plugin-catalog-only-write-preserves-root
    root models.json ino cold=4067823 warm=4067823 mtimeMs cold=1781500389539.2183 warm=1781500389539.2183
    ok
[5/5] unreadable-auth-store-fails-closed
    no-db outcome.kind=absent
    valid-store outcome.kind=present
    corrupt-db outcome.kind=unreadable
    corrupt-db fingerprint cacheable=false
    malformed-json-cell outcome.kind=unreadable
    malformed-json-cell fingerprint cacheable=false
    ok

All runtime assertions passed.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** All five runtime assertions passed (process exit 0). The source-fingerprint shim now reports `cacheable=false` for both corrupt SQLite and malformed JSON auth-store states, so catalog callers can decline persisted cache keys instead of reusing stale rows under a stable sentinel.
- **What was not tested:** A fully live multi-provider gateway boot is not exercised; the cache populate/drift/reconcile, no-create auth read, plugin-catalog-only write guard, and unreadable-auth fingerprint semantics are driven end-to-end against real on-disk state by the proof script above. Focused unit coverage pins the model-catalog and provider-catalog consumer behavior.

target.title: fix: prevent silent message loss from EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError
target.body:
## Summary

When the OpenAI SDK retried an `ECONNRESET` internally (default `maxRetries=2`), the session write lock was released during the retry window. Incoming steering messages could then modify the transcript without updating the fence, causing a fingerprint mismatch on `reacquireAfterPrompt()`. The resulting `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` was caught by `model-fallback.ts` which aborted the run, and the outer reply pipeline produced no user-visible output — the message was silently lost.

## Root Cause Chain

```
API ECONNRESET
  → OpenAI SDK default maxRetries=2, internal retry
  → session write lock released during retry window
  → incoming message arrives via queueHandle.queueMessage
  → activeSession.steer writes directly (bypassing withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites)
  → transcript changes without updating fence
  → original attempt reacquireAfterPrompt() fingerprint mismatch
  → EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError thrown
  → model-fallback.ts aborts the run
  → outer layer hits generic failure branch
  → assistantTexts/payloads never enter buildReplyPayloads()
  → message silently lost (user sees generic error or nothing)
```

## Fix

Five coordinated changes that address the bug at multiple layers:

### 1. Wrap steering in `withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites` (`attempt.ts`)
Steering writes that arrive during prompt lock release now go through `withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites`, keeping fence tracking consistent and preventing the fingerprint mismatch that triggers the takeover error.

### 2. Dedicated error handler for takeover (`agent-runner-execution.ts`, `failover-error.ts`)
Added `isEmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError()` export and a dedicated catch block in `runAgentTurnWithFallback`. Instead of falling into the generic failure branch (silent loss), users now receive a clear message asking them to resend. When a takeover wraps a preserved prompt error (billing, rate-limit, context-overflow), the original error is unwrapped and classified normally — only pure takeover errors return resend guidance.

### 3. Scope SDK retry disable to embedded prompt lock path (`attempt.ts`, `attempt.session-lock.ts`)
Extracted `installEmbeddedPromptRetryDefault` helper that wraps the session's `streamFn` with `{ maxRetries: 0, ...options }` — the `0` is a **default** that goes before the caller options spread, so `settings.retry.provider.maxRetries` (surfaced via `options.maxRetries` per the chain in `sdk.ts:360`) takes precedence when explicitly configured. Non-embedded callers retain the SDK default retry behavior (maxRetries=2).

### 4. Forward maxRetries through native OpenAI transport (`openai-transport-stream.ts`)
`buildOpenAISdkRequestOptions` previously accepted only `model` and `signal`, dropping the `maxRetries` injected by `installEmbeddedPromptRetryDefault`. Added `maxRetries` parameter and forwarded it through all three native transport call sites (Responses, Azure Responses, Completions). When `maxRetries` is the only option present, the function now returns an object instead of `undefined` so the SDK receives the override.

### 5. Phase tracking in `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` (`attempt.session-lock.ts`)
Added a `phase` field (`prompt_reacquire`, `cleanup`, `write`, `fence_check`) for better diagnostics when this error does occur. Backward compatible — the phase parameter is optional with existing call sites unchanged.

## Real behavior proof

> **Refreshed at current head `bf4c277c9b` (supersedes the v6 proof below, which targeted older head `2fc3ecee2d`).**

- **Behavior addressed**: A session takeover during the embedded prompt-lock release window must not silently drop the user's reply, and the injected embedded retry default must refresh with the latest configured `settings.retry.provider.maxRetries` across repeated installs (not freeze the first-captured value).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `bf4c277c9b`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, freshly installed deps (`pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran the affected suites at this head with `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs <suite>` — the organic session-takeover regression (real on-disk session + real local HTTP model server + real OpenAI-completions transport + real prompt-lock fence), the retry-default live-ref unit suite (V1–V5), and the reply-runtime regression — plus `tsgo:test:src` for typecheck.
- **Evidence after fix**: Copied live terminal output captured at head `bf4c277c9b`:
```
 ✓ surfaces resend guidance after an organic EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError  724ms
 ✓ src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts (72 tests) 141ms
      Tests  72 passed (72)
      Tests  205 passed (205)
 tsgo:test:src exits 0 with no errors
```
- **Observed result after fix**: The organic session takeover now surfaces explicit resend guidance ("⚠️ Your message was interrupted because new input arrived while the model was retrying a connection error. Please resend your message.") instead of silently dropping the reply; the retry-default wrapper reads the shared live ref at call time so a repeat install refreshes the next call (including downward refresh when the configured value drops); and typecheck is clean at this head.
- **What was not tested**: The live Feishu/DeepSeek ECONNRESET timing race that originally surfaced #87180 is not reproduced here; the takeover is reproduced deterministically via a real on-disk fence-drift, which exercises the same `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` path. The CI matrix on this head was still running at proof time.


### Current-head proof (`bf4c277c9b`)

- **Behavior addressed**: A session takeover during the embedded prompt-lock release window must not silently drop the user's reply, and the injected embedded retry default must refresh with the latest configured `settings.retry.provider.maxRetries` across repeated installs (not freeze the first-captured value).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `bf4c277c9b`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, freshly installed deps (`pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`).
- **Why refreshed**: The previous proof targeted an older head; the retry-default wrapper was reworked to read a shared live ref at call time (commit on this head), so proof is regenerated on the resulting head.

### 1. Organic session-takeover regression (real on-disk session + real local HTTP model server + real OpenAI-completions transport + real prompt-lock fence)
```
     ✓ surfaces resend guidance after an organic EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError  724ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  1 passed (1)
```
The takeover error is thrown organically by the real prompt-lock fence (a concurrent steering write rewrites the same session file mid-call), and `runAgentTurnWithFallback` surfaces resend guidance instead of dropping the reply.

### 2. Retry-default live-ref unit proof (the wrapper fix, V1–V5)
```
 ✓  agents  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts (72 tests) 141ms
      Tests  72 passed (72)
```
Covers: refresh through the buried wrapper after lock-release wraps it; call-time live read so a repeat install changes the next call; downward refresh when the configured value drops (the original bug's essence); shared ref identity across both install orders; and no wrapper stacking on repeated installs.

### 3. Reply-runtime regression (no behavior regression on the takeover catch path)
```
      Tests  205 passed (205)
```

### Typecheck
`tsgo:test:src` exits 0 with no errors at this head.

### What was not tested
The live Feishu/DeepSeek ECONNRESET timing race that originally surfaced #87180 is not reproduced here; the takeover is reproduced deterministically via a real on-disk fence-drift, which exercises the same `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` path. The CI matrix on this head was still running at proof time.


<details>
<summary>Earlier proof (v6, head 2fc3ecee2d) — historical</summary>


- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Silent message loss on session takeover during the released prompt-lock window. When the OpenAI SDK retried an `ECONNRESET` internally, an incoming steering write changed the transcript without updating the fence, raising `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError`, which `model-fallback.ts` aborted into a generic failure branch so the user saw no actionable output. After this fix the pure-takeover path returns explicit resend guidance, the SDK retry default is scoped to the embedded prompt-lock window (with configured `settings.retry.provider.maxRetries` taking precedence), and `maxRetries` is forwarded through the native OpenAI transport.
- **Real environment tested**: Clean Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (Node.js `v24.16.0`, Debian GNU/Linux 12 bookworm, aarch64) running the patched checkout at the current head commit `2fc3ecee2d`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Built the patched checkout in the container and ran the affected suites plus a real-setup organic takeover test (real on-disk session file + real local `node:http` model server on an `openai-completions` baseUrl + real transport + real fence) and code-path verification, as captured in the v6 proof log below.
- **Evidence after fix**: Copied live terminal output from the patched container at head `2fc3ecee2d` (full v6 log reproduced in the section below):

```
$ docker run --rm -v "$PWD:/app" -w /app node:24-bookworm bash /proof.sh
=== PR #89039 Real Behavior Proof (v6, current head) ===
Environment: Docker container, v24.16.0, aarch64, "Debian GNU/Linux 12 (bookworm)"
Commit: 2fc3ecee2d
--- A: Real-setup organic session-takeover proof (resend guidance) ---
 ✓ surfaces resend guidance after an organic EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError 1456ms
AFTER-fix user-facing payload text (exact):
⚠️ Your message was interrupted because new input arrived while the model was retrying a connection error. Please resend your message.
BEFORE-fix user-facing payload text (exact, parent 86150a3e51):
⚠️ Something went wrong while processing your request. Please try again, or use /new to start a fresh session.
=== PROOF COMPLETE @ 2fc3ecee2d ===
```
- **Observed result after fix**: The organic session takeover now surfaces a specific, actionable resend message (the after-fix payload line above) instead of the generic non-actionable failure seen at the parent; the configured provider retry survives through `sdk.ts` to the native SDK request options; the native transport forwards `maxRetries`; and the retry-default installer is idempotent with caller > configured > 0 precedence.
- **What was not tested**: No live multi-user deployment against a real upstream provider with an actual network-level `ECONNRESET` was run. The takeover proof uses a real on-disk session file, a real local HTTP model server, the real transport, and the real fence (organic error), with only the upstream provider socket and the "second user" steering write synthesized; the supplementary transport proof relies on unit/loopback tests in a real Docker container.

### Current-head proof (commit `2fc3ecee2d`, Docker `node:24-bookworm`)

Refreshed at the latest head, which adds the configured-provider-retry precedence. Run in a clean Docker container (`node:24-bookworm`, Node.js `v24.16.0`, Debian GNU/Linux 12 bookworm, aarch64):

```
=== PR #89039 Real Behavior Proof (v6, current head) ===
Environment: Docker container, v24.16.0, aarch64, "Debian GNU/Linux 12 (bookworm)"
Commit: 2fc3ecee2d

--- A: Real-setup organic session-takeover proof (resend guidance) ---
 ✓ auto-reply  agent-runner-session-takeover.real.test.ts (1 test)
   ✓ surfaces resend guidance after an organic EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError 1456ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  1 passed (1)

--- B: Embedded prompt retry default — idempotence + configured-override precedence ---
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  51 passed (51)

--- C: Configured provider retry survives through sdk.ts to native SDK options ---
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)

--- D: Transport forwards maxRetries (native OpenAI) + failover classification ---
 Test Files  1 passed (1)    Tests  83 passed (83)
 Test Files  1 passed (1)    Tests  241 passed (241)

--- E: Code-path verification at current head ---
installEmbeddedPromptRetryDefault accepts configured default:
1088:  options?: { maxRetries?: number },
1098:  const defaultMaxRetries = options?.maxRetries ?? 0;
Install site passes configured provider retry:
3994:              maxRetries: settingsManager.getProviderRetrySettings().maxRetries,

=== PROOF COMPLETE @ 2fc3ecee2d ===
```

This confirms at the current head: the organic session-takeover path still yields visible resend guidance (A); the retry-default installer is idempotent and applies the configured-override precedence caller > configured > 0 (B); a configured `settings.retry.provider.maxRetries` survives through `sdk.ts` to the native SDK request options (C, the new regression); and the native transport forwards `maxRetries` (D).

### Real session-takeover path (organic error, real transport + real fence)

This proof drives the actual user-visible takeover path end to end and captures the real user-facing reply, rather than relying only on focused unit tests. Committed as `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-takeover.real.test.ts`.

**What is real (not hand-built):**
- A real on-disk session transcript file in a temp dir.
- A real local `node:http` server as the model `baseUrl` (`api: openai-completions`) that announces in-flight, then blocks before finishing the SSE body.
- The real `openai-completions` transport doing real HTTP I/O.
- The real embedded prompt-lock controller + fence (`createEmbeddedAttemptSessionLockController`), wired through the real `installEmbeddedPromptRetryDefault` / `installPromptSubmissionLockRelease` helpers — the exact helpers `attempt.ts` uses in production.
- The `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` is thrown **organically** by `assertSessionFileFence("prompt_reacquire")` when a concurrent steering write changes the session-file fingerprint during the released-lock window — it is **not** constructed by hand.
- The real `isNonProviderRuntimeCoordinationError` classification and the real `runAgentTurnWithFallback` catch branch producing the user-facing payload.

**Mechanism (deterministic Promise handshakes, no sleeps):**
1. A real temp session file is created with one session line.
2. On the first model request the mock server resolves a `firstRequestReceived` deferred (the model call is in-flight), then blocks on a `releaseServer` deferred before writing the body.
3. `installPromptSubmissionLockRelease` calls `releaseForPrompt()` at the start of the model call (opening the takeover window) and drains the real transport stream, so the released-lock window spans the real HTTP block.
4. While blocked, the test performs a concurrent "second user" steering write to the **same** real session file, changing its fingerprint.
5. `releaseServer` resolves; the server finishes the SSE body; the wrapped `finally{}` calls `reacquireAfterPrompt()` → `assertSessionFileFence("prompt_reacquire")`.
6. The fingerprint mismatch trips the fence → `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` thrown organically → real classification → real `runAgentTurnWithFallback` catch.

**Before / after (run at this PR's parent `86150a3e51` in a separate worktree; the session-lock + fence code is byte-identical between parent and HEAD, so the organic error reproduces identically — the only difference is the runner's new catch branch):**

```
AFTER fix (HEAD) — vitest verbose
 RUN  v4.1.7
 ✓ |auto-reply| agent-runner-session-takeover.real.test.ts > surfaces resend guidance after an organic EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError 487ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  1 passed (1)
AFTER-fix user-facing payload text (exact):
⚠️ Your message was interrupted because new input arrived while the model was retrying a connection error. Please resend your message.

BEFORE fix (parent 86150a3e51, separate worktree) — vitest verbose
 RUN  v4.1.7
 ✓ |auto-reply| agent-runner-session-takeover.real.test.ts > BEFORE FIX: organic EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError yields no resend guidance 483ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  1 passed (1)
BEFORE-fix user-facing payload text (exact):
⚠️ Something went wrong while processing your request. Please try again, or use /new to start a fresh session.
```

**What this proves:** an organic session takeover during a real released-lock model call produces, after this fix, a specific actionable resend message instead of the generic non-actionable failure the user saw before. Honest note: at this PR's immediate parent the before-fix payload is the **generic** failure text shown above (not literal empty output); the originally reported "silent message loss" is the production symptom of that non-actionable failure path. The change at this seam converts the generic failure into specific resend guidance so the user knows their steering message must be resent.

### Supplementary: transport + retry-suppression proof (Docker `node:24-bookworm`)

- **Behavior addressed**: Silent message loss when SDK retries race with session takeover → visible resend guidance for pure takeover, correct error classification for wrapped prompt errors, provider retry settings respected, maxRetries forwarded through native OpenAI transport
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:24-bookworm`, Node.js `v24.16.0`, Debian GNU/Linux 12 bookworm, aarch64) running patched checkout at commit `80bbe803e2`
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `buildOpenAISdkRequestOptions` only accepts `model` and `signal` — it drops `maxRetries`. Embedded prompt lock window injects `maxRetries: 0` but the native OpenAI transport never forwards it to the SDK, so SDK retries (default `maxRetries=2`) still occur during the released lock window, widening the race. Additionally, `queueMessage` steering writes bypass `withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites`, causing fingerprint mismatch → `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` → generic failure branch → silent message loss.
- **Exact steps and commands run after this patch**:

```
$ docker run --rm -e CI=true -v "$(pwd):/app" -w /app node:24-bookworm bash /proof.sh
=== PR #89039 Real Behavior Proof (v5) ===
Environment: Docker container, v24.16.0, aarch64, "Debian GNU/Linux 12 (bookworm)"
Commit: 80bbe803e2

target.title: fix(parallel): bound MCP success response body read
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`postMcp` in `parallel-mcp-search.runtime.ts` reads the HTTP response body in a
single ternary expression — but only the **error** branch is bounded:

```ts
text: response.ok
  ? await response.text()                                                   // ← unbounded
  : await readResponseTextLimited(response, PARALLEL_MCP_ERROR_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES),  // ← 8 KiB cap
```

The asymmetry is visible on adjacent lines in the same ternary: `readResponseTextLimited`
is already imported for the error path, yet `response.text()` is used on the
success path with no size guard. A misbehaving or hostile Parallel AI MCP
endpoint can stream an arbitrarily large JSON-RPC body into memory on the
success path, causing OOM — while the identical code one line below already
knows to cap the error body.

This follows the same campaign as Alix-007's `#95103`/`#95218` response-limit
fixes, but applied to the MCP transport layer rather than provider JSON
endpoints.

## Changes

* `extensions/parallel/src/parallel-mcp-search.runtime.ts`:
  - Adds constant `PARALLEL_MCP_SUCCESS_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES = 16 * 1024 * 1024`
    (16 MiB — same default as `readProviderJsonResponse`; matches expected
    MCP tool-call payload sizes while guarding against runaway bodies).
  - Replaces `await response.text()` in the success branch of `postMcp` with
    `await readResponseTextLimited(response, PARALLEL_MCP_SUCCESS_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES)`.
  - No new imports — `readResponseTextLimited` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`
    is already present for the error branch in the same expression.
  - No test changes needed — test file already uses real `Response` objects
    and all 17 tests pass unchanged.

## Real behavior proof

* **Behavior addressed:** A Parallel AI MCP endpoint can no longer stream an
  arbitrarily large success body into memory; the success-branch read is now
  capped at 16 MiB, matching the error branch's capped pattern, and the stream
  is cancelled on overflow.
* **Real environment tested:** A real `node:http` `createServer` bound to
  `127.0.0.1`, streaming a 24 MiB body in 64 KiB chunks with **no
  Content-Length** header, driving the real exported `readResponseTextLimited`
  helper on Node v22.22.0.
* **Exact steps:**
  1. Boot server; `GET /huge-success` (200) and `GET /huge-error` (400) stream
     24 MiB each; `GET /small` returns a valid MCP JSON-RPC response.
  2. Drive `readResponseTextLimited(res, 16 MiB)` against `/huge-success` and
     assert text length ≤ 16 MiB and bytes-on-wire stayed near cap.
  3. Drive `readResponseTextLimited(res, 8 KiB)` against `/huge-error` and
     assert text length ≤ 8 KiB and bytes-on-wire stayed near cap.
  4. **Negative control**: run OLD `await response.text()` against
     `/huge-success` and measure total bytes.
  5. Drive against `/small` and assert the JSON-RPC payload is returned intact.
* **Evidence after fix:**
  * Success cap: text length **16,777,216 bytes** (16 MiB); server sent
    **18,677,760 bytes** — near cap, not the full 24 MiB body.
  * Error cap: text length **8,192 bytes** (8 KiB); server sent
    **589,824 bytes** — near cap, not the full 24 MiB body.
  * Negative control: unbounded `response.text()` pulled the **full
    25,165,824 bytes**, proving both caps are load-bearing.
  * Small case: payload returned intact with `"jsonrpc"` field present.
* **Observed result:** `ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED`.
  In-repo Vitest: **1 file, 17 tests — all passed**, including the existing
  `"bounds initialize error bodies without using response.text()"` regression.
* **What was not tested:** Live Parallel AI MCP endpoint calls. The proof
  measures bytes-on-wire and text truncation, which is the load-bearing signal.

## Evidence

```
[case 1] oversized SUCCESS body (200), cap=16 MiB
  ok: bounded at 16 MiB (got 16777216 bytes) — true
  ok: bytes on wire near cap, not full 24 MiB (sent 18677760) — true

[case 2] oversized ERROR body (400), cap=8 KiB
  ok: bounded at 8 KiB (got 8192 bytes) — true
  ok: bytes on wire near cap, not full 24 MiB (sent 589824) — true

[negative control] OLD unbounded response.text()
  ok: unbounded read pulled full ~24 MiB (sent 25165824) — true

[case 3] normal small MCP JSON response
  ok: small response returned intact — true
  ok: content matches original — true

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  17 passed (17)
   Start at  01:09:24
   Duration  1.23s
```

Proof script: `scripts/proof-parallel-mcp-bound.mjs`
Node version: v22.22.0

**Label**: security

AI-assisted.

target.title: Doctor: expose state integrity findings
target.body:
## Summary

- Exposes `core/doctor/state-integrity` as a structured Doctor health finding surface.
- Maps existing state directory, storage-class, config permission, and runtime state-directory diagnostics into stable `HealthFinding` output.
- Adds dry-run repair effects for the state-integrity issues while leaving real mutations in the legacy `noteStateIntegrity` Doctor path.
- Preserves independent config-file permission findings even when the OpenClaw state directory is missing.
- Does not add or change config, plugin, or public SDK contract surface.

This is the next narrow structured Doctor rule slice after session artifacts. It intentionally keeps the existing Doctor state-integrity implementation authoritative for real `doctor` / `doctor --fix` behavior.

## Stack

Rebased onto current `main` at `8bc069f76f` (`fix(outbound): preserve narrowed delivery target type`). Current branch head is `24d45fc5ae` (`fix(doctor): preserve config permission findings`).

Conflict resolution was limited to `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`; the contribution ID assertion now keeps the already-merged sandbox, gateway-services, config-audit, session-transcripts, session-snapshots, and state-integrity IDs.

## Validation

Current head `24d45fc5ae`:

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/commands/doctor-state-integrity.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts --reporter=dot` - 80 tests passed, 1 Windows-only permission-mode test skipped, across 2 Vitest shards
- `pnpm exec oxfmt.CMD --check src/commands/doctor-state-integrity.test.ts src/commands/doctor-state-integrity.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint.CMD src/commands/doctor-state-integrity.test.ts src/commands/doctor-state-integrity.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `git diff --check`

Earlier on the same rebased source diff before the test-only expectation amend:

- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `node scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs --check`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Structured Doctor state-integrity findings now surface existing missing state-directory diagnostics through `core/doctor/state-integrity`, and the structured repair adapter reports a dry-run effect for the same issue while real mutation remains owned by the legacy Doctor path.

Real environment tested:
Windows source checkout at PR head `24d45fc5ae`, using isolated temp `HOME` / `USERPROFILE`, isolated missing `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, `NO_COLOR=1`, and `OPENCLAW_DISABLE_AUTO_UPDATE=1`. The CLI proof uses the public source CLI path. The dry-run effect proof uses the structured Doctor repair source harness because command-wide dry-run report output is the later #84472 surface.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Created an isolated temp home and intentionally missing state directory.
2. Ran `node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/state-integrity`.
3. Ran `node --import tsx -e "...resolveDoctorContributionHealthChecks(); check.detect(...); check.repair({...dryRun:true}, findings)"` for the same isolated missing state directory.

Evidence after fix:
Public CLI structured finding, redacted:

```text
PROOF_ROOT=<TEMP_PROOF_ROOT>
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<TEMP_PROOF_ROOT>\missing-state
{"ok":false,"checksRun":1,"checksSkipped":27,"findings":[{"checkId":"core/doctor/state-integrity","severity":"error","message":"State directory is missing. Sessions, credentials, logs, and config are stored there.","path":"<TEMP_PROOF_ROOT>\\missing-state","fixHint":"Run `openclaw doctor --fix` to create the state directory."}]}
EXIT_CODE=1
```

Structured dry-run repair effect source harness, redacted:

```json
{
  "findings": [
    {
      "checkId": "core/doctor/state-integrity",
      "severity": "error",
      "message": "State directory is missing. Sessions, credentials, logs, and config are stored there.",
      "path": "<TEMP_PROOF_ROOT>\\missing-state",
      "fixHint": "Run `openclaw doctor --fix` to create the state directory."
    }
  ],
  "repair": {
    "status": "repaired",
    "changes": [],
    "effects": [
      {
        "kind": "state",
        "action": "would-create-state-dir",
        "target": "<TEMP_PROOF_ROOT>\\missing-state",
        "dryRunSafe": false
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix:
The public lint command ran exactly one check, emitted one `error` finding for `core/doctor/state-integrity`, and exited 1. The structured repair adapter reported the matching `would-create-state-dir` effect without mutating the missing state directory.

What was not tested:
POSIX config permission live CLI output was not tested on this Windows host; that branch remains covered by the POSIX-only regression because Windows intentionally skips mode diagnostics. Command-wide dry-run report output was not tested because that public reporting surface is planned in #84472, not this PR.

## Notes

The rebased diff is 4 files changed, +458/-1. The latest update fixed the POSIX-only missing-state-dir regression expectation to match the actual mapper text.

target.title: fix(browser): notify agent when click triggers download
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

- When a browser action triggers a file download, the `/act` response returned only `{result: "triggered"}` — no download path or filename
- The agent had no way to know a file was saved, causing it to retry the action (see #93250)
- Root cause: `saveAs` is async and can arrive after the action returns; the old code had no mechanism to wait for or report download completion

Fixes #93250

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.ts:283-336` | Add `beginActionDownloadCaptureOnPage()` — per-action download capture with `drain()` + optional `graceMs` |
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.ts:246-264` | Extract `saveUnmanagedDownload()` — returns `{suggestedFilename, savedPath}` |
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.ts:778-791` | Broadcast download to all active captures (not just newest), with `managedSave.catch()` always attached to prevent unhandled rejections |
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.interactions.ts:1729-1774` | Wire capture + drain into `executeActViaPlaywright`, returning `downloads` in result |
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.interactions.ts:1697-1707` | Add `actionDownloadGraceMs()` — 500ms grace for click/clickCoords/batch/evaluate |
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/agent.act.ts:684-706` | Propagate `downloads` into `/act` JSON responses |
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.ts:35-38` | Type `downloads` in `BrowserActResponse` |
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.test.ts` | +7 test cases: broadcast, sequential isolation, multi-download, dispose isolation, double-dispose, zero-grace |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Fix for issue #93250: after a browser act request triggers a download, the `/act` JSON response now includes `{downloads: {count, recent: [{suggestedFilename, savedPath}]}}` so the agent can see where the file was saved and stop retrying.

**Real setup tested**: Node.js v24.13.1 on Linux x86_64, Google Chrome 143.0.7499.169

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:
```bash
# Start Chrome CDP and HTTP test page
google-chrome --remote-debugging-port=19222 --no-first-run --user-data-dir=/tmp/chrome-act &
python3 -m http.server 19990 --directory /tmp/download-test &

# Call executeActViaPlaywright (same code path as gateway POST /act):
# evaluateEnabled: true + action: { kind: "evaluate", fn: ... }
node e2e-act-proof.mjs
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
$ curl --noproxy '*' -s -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" http://127.0.0.1:19990/index.html
HTTP 200

$ curl --noproxy '*' -s http://127.0.0.1:19222/json/version | node -e "..."
Chrome/143.0.7499.169

POST /act { kind: 'evaluate', fn: ... }

══════════════════════════════════════════
  POST /act RESPONSE (agent-visible JSON)
══════════════════════════════════════════
{
  "result": "triggered",
  "downloads": {
    "count": 1,
    "recent": [
      {
        "suggestedFilename": "act-proof-download.txt",
        "savedPath": "/tmp/openclaw/downloads/9af832ae-8b1a-4674-88ef-ad9f1f542a25-act-proof-download.txt"
      }
    ]
  }
}

$ node -e "const fs=require('fs');console.log('exists:',fs.existsSync('/tmp/openclaw/downloads/9af832ae-...-act-proof-download.txt'),'content:',fs.readFileSync('/tmp/openclaw/downloads/9af832ae-...-act-proof-download.txt','utf-8').trim())"
exists: true content: OpenClaw /act download proof - PR 93307
```

**Observed result after the fix**: The `/act` response JSON now includes a `downloads` object with `count` and `recent[{suggestedFilename, savedPath}]`. The saved file exists on disk with correct contents. This is the exact JSON payload the agent receives.

**What was not tested**: Cross-platform behavior (macOS, Windows).

## Tests and validation

All 21 `pw-session` tests pass — broadcast-to-all, sequential isolation, multi-download, dispose isolation, double-dispose, zero-grace.

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-browser.config.ts \
  browser/src/browser/pw-session.test.ts
# Test Files  1 passed (1)
#      Tests  21 passed (21)
```

*AI-assisted: built with Claude Code*

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: perf: avoid event-loop stall during embedded_run bootstrap-context
target.body:
## Summary

Fix event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context that block the event loop for 14-22 seconds, causing message loss.

### Root causes addressed

1. **Synchronous file I/O** — `readFileSync`/`closeSync` in workspace file loading blocks the event loop while reading root bootstrap files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, etc.)
2. **Unbounded blocking glob walks** — `fs.glob` for extra bootstrap files traverses the entire workspace tree synchronously inside Node, including `.git`/`node_modules` directories, without yielding to the event loop
3. **Uninterrupted hook chains** — `triggerInternalHook` executes all handlers back-to-back without yielding

### Changes

**`src/agents/workspace.ts`**
- Replace sync `readFileSync`/`closeSync` with callback-based async `readFdToString`/`closeFdAsync`
- Parallelize root bootstrap file reads via `Promise.all` (previously serial `for` loop)
- Replace `fs.glob` with an async traversal walker that yields via `setImmediate` every 256 entries, so a sparse pattern like `**/AGENTS.md` over a large workspace never blocks the event loop. The walker performs no directory pruning — it walks the full tree and returns every file matching the configured patterns, with no traversal cutoff and no artificial match cap; the downstream bootstrap character budget owns content limiting.
- Suppress `closeFdAsync` rejections to avoid masking read errors on NFS/FUSE

**`src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`**
- Add `yieldBetweenHandlers` option with `setImmediate` yield between hook handlers
- Add per-handler timing instrumentation with handler index reporting
- Log warnings for slow handlers exceeding 500ms threshold

**`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`**
- Instrument bootstrap-context substages (`workspace-file-load`, `hook-overrides`) with `performance.now` timing
- Log substage durations when total bootstrap-context exceeds 2 seconds
- Enable `yieldBetweenHandlers` for `agent:bootstrap` hook triggers

**`src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts`** / **`src/agents/bootstrap-hooks.ts`**
- Support new async workspace file loading and timing callback interfaces

### Tests

- `workspace.test.ts` — 3 new tests for async fd read (happy path, cache hit, fd close on failure)
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` — every matching file returned without an artificial cap (no directory pruning), and a regression test asserting a late-appearing match in a large tree is still loaded (no traversal cutoff)
- `handler.test.ts` — no truncation warning is emitted for large glob scans
- `internal-hooks.test.ts` — 3 new tests for yield-between-handlers ordering, handler timing reporting, and slow handler warning

All existing tests pass without modification.

### Performance impact

- Root bootstrap file loading: serial sync → parallel async (estimated 3-5x speedup for 8 files)
- Glob walks: synchronous/blocking → async with periodic `setImmediate` yields (prevents multi-second event-loop stalls on large workspaces). The walk completes fully; all configured matches are kept, and the existing bootstrap character-budget truncation handles content limiting.
- Hook chains: uninterrupted → yielded (prevents monopolizing the event loop during bootstrap)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context loading caused by synchronous I/O and a blocking `fs.glob` walk over large workspaces, which blocks the loop and drops messages. This change makes the glob walk async/yielding so the loop stays responsive, while preserving every configured `bootstrap-extra-files` glob match (the earlier iteration of this branch added a hard 20,000-entry traversal cutoff that silently dropped late matches — that cutoff is removed here).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout on macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, run via `tsx` against the real exported `loadExtraBootstrapFiles` from `src/agents/workspace.ts`. Before/after captured from two git commits on the PR branch: before = `5849e709f4` (walker with the 20,000-entry cutoff), after = `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed).
- **Real environment scenario**: A workspace of ~26,100 filesystem entries (a 3-tier tree, 30×30×28 filler files) with a single configured-glob target `AGENTS.md` planted in the subtree the depth-first walker drains last, so the target is only reached after more than 20,000 visited entries. A 5ms repeating timer runs during the load as an event-loop liveness probe (steady ticks ⇒ the loop never stalled).
- **Failing proof before fix**: commit `5849e709f4` (cutoff present):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 82.9ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 16 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 0
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: false
  RESULT: late configured glob match was DROPPED (pre-fix behavior)
  ```
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  # against the patched checkout (commit 5ce395d645) a script that builds the
  # ~26,100-entry workspace, starts a 5ms liveness timer, then calls the real
  # loadExtraBootstrapFiles(root, ["**/AGENTS.md"]) and checks the deep match:
  npx tsx proof-live.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output from `node`/`tsx` against commit `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 99.4ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 20 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 1
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: true
  RESULT: configured glob match beyond old cutoff is PRESERVED; event loop stayed live
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the cutoff removed, the configured `**/AGENTS.md` match located past the old 20,000-entry frontier is now loaded (1 file returned vs 0 before in the terminal output above), so existing broad `bootstrap-extra-files` configs no longer silently lose AGENTS.md/TOOLS.md context. The liveness timer kept ticking throughout the async walk (20 ticks during the ~99ms load), confirming the periodic `setImmediate` yields — not the cutoff — are what keep the event loop responsive.
- **What was not tested**: No full live `embedded_run` with active channel connections was driven end-to-end; this proof exercises the real glob-resolution code path and event-loop liveness directly rather than through a connected channel. The focused regression coverage lives in `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` and `handler.test.ts`.

Closes #87509

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix(discord): disable ws 8.21.0 receiver part limits for gateway sockets
target.body:
## Summary

ws 8.21.0 introduced `maxBufferedChunks` and `maxFragments` receiver limits (commit `[security] Limit retained message parts`) that can trigger `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and close connections with code 1008 under certain traffic patterns.

This PR explicitly sets both limits to `0` (disabled) for Discord gateway WebSocket connections, restoring the ws 8.20.0 behavior for the trusted Discord gateway peer while preserving the security fix for all other WebSocket usage.

## Changes

- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.ts`**: Base class `createWebSocket()` now passes `maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0` with a `ws.ClientOptions` cast (since `@types/ws` 8.18.1 does not yet expose these fields).
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.ts`**: Extended `DiscordGatewayWebSocketCtor` type to include the new options; monitor gateway creation path passes the same disabled limits.
- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.test.ts`**: Added test verifying base class WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.test.ts`**: Added test verifying monitor plugin WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.proxy.test.ts`**: Updated two existing exact-match assertions to include the new options.

## Why `0` instead of raising the limits

- ws 8.21.0 treats `0` as disabled (`receiver.js:61`, `receiver.js:98`: `if (this._maxBufferedChunks > 0 && ...)`), matching the pre-8.21.0 behavior exactly.
- Discord gateway is a trusted peer; the security limits are designed to protect against untrusted senders.
- Choosing an arbitrary higher threshold would be fragile without real production traffic data to calibrate against.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: ws 8.21.0's retained-part receiver limits (`maxFragments` default 131072 / `maxBufferedChunks` default 1048576) abort a Discord gateway socket with `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` / close `1008`; this PR sets both to `0` (disabled) on the Discord gateway sockets, restoring ws 8.20.0 behavior for that trusted peer.
- **Real environment tested**: Live Discord gateway connection from a Docker `node:22-bookworm-slim` container (Node `v22.22.3`) with the repo-pinned `ws@8.21.0` installed exactly, using a real bot token (redacted), connecting to `wss://gateway.discord.gg/?v=10&encoding=json`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: build a `node:22-bookworm-slim` image with `npm install ws@8.21.0`, then run a client that constructs the gateway socket with this patch's options (`new ws.WebSocket(url, { handshakeTimeout: 30000, maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0 })`), completes the HELLO/IDENTIFY/READY handshake against the real Discord gateway, sends heartbeats, holds a 60s stable window, then closes with code 1000.
- **Evidence after fix**: Live Discord gateway run with this patch's options applied (token, `session_id`, and `resume_gateway_url` redacted):

```
[env] node=v22.22.3 ws=8.21.0
[discord] connecting wss://gateway.discord.gg (maxBufferedChunks:0, maxFragments:0)
[discord] open
[discord] HELLO heartbeat_interval=41250ms
[discord] IDENTIFY sent (token redacted)
[discord] READY received (session_id/resume_gateway_url redacted)
[discord] HEARTBEAT_ACK #1
[discord] stable window 60000ms complete
[discord] close code=1000 reason=<empty>
[result] PASS live: READY + 1 heartbeat ACK; no WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS during stable window
```

Supplemental deterministic mechanism repro against a local ws 8.21.0 gateway streaming one message in 131073 fragments (one over the client default `maxFragments`), exercising the same `ws.WebSocket` receiver path:

```
# default ws.WebSocket (ws 8.21 defaults)
[before] open
[before] error code=WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS message=Too many message fragments
[before] close code=1006 reason=<empty>

# this patch's options applied
[after] open
[after] message received: 131073 bytes - NO receiver-limit close
```

- **Observed result after fix**: On the real Discord gateway, with the disabled limits applied, the connection reaches `READY`, exchanges heartbeats, and stays connected through the 60s window with no `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and no `1008` close; the only close is the intentional client-initiated `1000`. The deterministic repro confirms the same constructor options remove the receiver-limit close that the default ws 8.21 client hits.
- **What was not tested**: the live run confirms a real gateway session stays healthy with the patched options, but does not force Discord's production fragmentation pattern on demand, does not exercise the `zlib-stream` transport, and does not drive the full OpenClaw plugin wiring end-to-end. The deterministic repro proves the ws 8.21 retained-part mechanism and that this patch's options disable the exact receiver checks, but uses a local peer rather than Discord's live framing.

## Tests

- `gateway.test.ts`: 25 passed
- `gateway-plugin.test.ts`: 17 passed  
- `provider.proxy.test.ts`: 21 passed
- TypeScript check (tsgo): clean

Fixes #88330

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix: recover terminal session status on visible inbound turns
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86827 — group chat sessions stuck in `failed` status silently drop all subsequent messages.

When a group chat session enters a terminal status (`failed`, `timeout`, or `killed`), the reply dispatch path returns a successful no-op (`queuedFinal: false`, zero dispatch counts) without starting an AI turn or delivering any user-visible error. All subsequent messages are silently discarded until a manual `/new` reset.

## Root Cause

Three independent mechanisms conspire to create the silent drop:

1. **Session state persistence**: `initSessionState()` reuses the existing session entry with its terminal `status` field intact, passing it downstream unchanged.
2. **Reply operation locking**: A stale `ReplyOperation` from the failed run remains registered in `replyRunRegistry`, causing `admitReplyTurn()` to return `skipped` with reason `active-run`.
3. **No fallback or diagnostic**: The `skipped` admission produces a valid dispatch result with zero counts, and no warning is logged — the message simply vanishes.

## Changes

### 1. Auto-recover terminal sessions (`session.ts`)

When a **visible** (non-cron/heartbeat) inbound message hits a session with recoverable terminal status, automatically clear the stale lifecycle fields (`status`, `startedAt`, `endedAt`, `runtimeMs`, `abortedLastRun`) while **preserving the `sessionId` and transcript continuity** — no session rotation.

### 2. Clear stale reply operations with race guard (`dispatch-from-config.ts`)

When `admitReplyTurn()` returns `skipped` with reason `active-run` but the persisted session is in terminal status, force-clear the stale reply operation via `forceClearReplyRunBySessionId()` and retry admission **once**. This breaks the deadlock without risking infinite retry loops.

**Concurrent recovery guard**: After a recovery turn clears the proven stale leftover and admits its own fresh operation, it is marked with a `terminalRecovery` flag. If a second visible message arrives against the same terminal snapshot before the first recovery clears the store state, the force-clear branch now skips operations marked as in-flight terminal recoveries. This prevents a sibling recovery turn from killing a fresh recovery that is already in flight, letting concurrent turns fall through to normal busy/wait handling instead.

### 3. Agent API path (`agent.ts`)

Mirror the same terminal recovery logic in the agent API dispatch path so agent API requests also clear terminal state for visible requests.

### 4. Diagnostics (`kernel.ts`, `types.ts`)

Add a structured warning log when a visible channel turn dispatch completes with `queuedFinal: false` and zero tool/block/final counts — a sentinel for future silent message drops. Excludes system turns (heartbeat/cron/exec) and observe-only admissions.

## Real behavior proof

Captured by driving the **real** runtime modules (`dispatchReplyFromConfig`, `initSessionState`, `reply-run-registry`) with verbose logging on, **no vitest and no module mocks** — only the production parameters the Gateway supplies plus a real on-disk JSON session store. A before/after contrast was produced by running the identical driver in a pre-fix worktree.

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A group-chat session left in a terminal lifecycle status (`failed`/`timeout`/`killed`) silently drops every later message — the stale lifecycle entry plus a leftover "active" reply operation block turn admission, so a subsequent visible inbound message gets no AI turn and no reply (#86827).
- **Real environment tested**: Compiled branch source at HEAD `ac52eb0b24`; macOS 15.3.1 (Darwin 24.3.0, arm64), Node `v24.10.0`. Product built with `pnpm build:docker` (exit 0); the recovery log string is present in compiled `dist/`. Evidence captured via `tsx` driving the real in-process dispatch/session/registry path. Before/after contrast run at pre-fix commit `8de37e1ce4` (parent of the first PR commit).
- **Failing proof before fix** (`8de37e1ce4`, identical driver + input): real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible group turn on a `status="failed"` session with a stale running reply op —
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  [dispatch] dispatch TIMED-OUT (no reply within 8s): visible turn stuck behind the stale terminal reply op -> SILENT DROP
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = null
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = true
  ```
  And real `initSessionState()` against an on-disk store: `RESULT: STILL-TERMINAL — session entry remains status=failed after a visible inbound turn`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm build:docker
  export OPENCLAW_HOME=$(mktemp -d) OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 \
         OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts   # drives real dispatchReplyFromConfig()
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_proof_driver.mts      # drives real initSessionState() + registry
  ```
  The driver seeds a real session store with a terminal `failed` group entry, registers a real stale running reply operation, then calls the real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible inbound group message.
- **Evidence after fix (`ac52eb0b24`)**: copied live console output from running the real in-process driver `node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts` (drives the production `dispatchReplyFromConfig()`); redacted runtime log:
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  dispatch-from-config: cleared stale active reply operation for terminal session agent:main:telegram:group:<redacted>
  [dispatch] dispatch returned: {"queuedFinal":true,"counts":{"tool":0,"block":0,"final":1}}
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = {"kind":"failed","code":"run_failed"}
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = false
  ```
  Real session lifecycle: `initSessionState -> isNewSession=false sessionId=<same> abortedLastRun=false`, and `AFTER turn -> status=undefined ... RESULT: RECOVERED — terminal lifecycle cleared, same sessionId reused (transcript preserved)`.
- **Observed result after fix**: A visible inbound message to a terminal session (1) emits the new recovery log, (2) force-fails the stale leftover op and frees the registry (`isActive=false`), (3) produces a real queued reply (`queuedFinal:true, counts.final:1`) instead of the pre-fix zero-count silent drop / indefinite block, and (4) clears the persisted terminal lifecycle fields while reusing the same `sessionId` (transcript preserved). The pre-fix checkout, given identical input, leaves the session `failed` and the turn stuck/dropped.
- **What was not tested**: The full Docker image build failed at the `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` layer due to transient npm-registry CDN errors fetching optional native packages (network, not OOM and not code), so no end-to-end live channel socket round-trip was performed; the proof drives the real in-process dispatch/session/registry runtime that a channel turn funnels into. The `agent.ts` Gateway RPC path and the `kernel.ts` zero-count warning were source-verified but not separately runtime-exercised here. Concurrency races (sibling visible-turn recovery, heartbeat non-clobber) are covered by the added unit tests. Architecture: arm64 (macOS) only; CI truth is Linux x64 Node 24.

## Tests

- **session.test.ts**: Verify failed group sessions recover without rotating the transcript
- **dispatch-from-config.test.ts**: Verify stale active reply operations are cleared for terminal sessions and admission is retried; verify two concurrent visible turns against the same terminal snapshot do not kill each other's recovery operations
- **agent.test.ts**: Verify terminal failed agent API sessions recover without session rotation; updated existing failed-session tests to verify terminal state clearing
- **kernel.test.ts**: Verify zero-count visible dispatch warning is emitted
- **agent-runner-execution.test.ts / agent-runner-memory.test.ts**: Updated mock `ReplyOperation` stubs with new `terminalRecovery` field and `markTerminalRecovery` method

All focused tests pass across 2 Vitest shards (512 cases).

target.title: test(plugins): cover filtered bundle MCP servers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR tightens the existing compatible-bundle MCP path for bundles that need a narrower default tool surface.

- Adds regression coverage that a Codex-compatible bundle `.mcp.json` preserves `toolFilter.include`, `toolFilter.exclude`, and a relative bundle data root.
- Documents bundle MCP tool filters on the plugin-bundles page alongside the existing transport and tool-naming guidance.
- Keeps scope limited to bundle MCP loading/docs; runtime filtering behavior already exists and is covered separately.

Reviewers should focus on whether the bundle MCP docs accurately describe the existing runtime behavior and whether the new compatible-bundle regression is useful.

## Linked context

No issue filed.

Related precedent investigated: bundle MCP support and follow-up coverage in prior OpenClaw PRs such as #48611, #71287, #79686, and #88399. This PR follows that existing bundle MCP seam.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Compatible bundles that contribute MCP servers can ship a narrow default tool surface through `toolFilter.include` and `toolFilter.exclude`; OpenClaw docs now describe that bundle path and a regression test proves the loader preserves the filter and relative app root.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `test/filtered-bundle-mcp`, commit `d5bffb3dc0`, with an isolated temporary `HOME`, `OPENCLAW_HOME`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temporary Codex-compatible bundle containing `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, `.mcp.json`, `skills/`, and `apps/`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins install --link <temp>/vision-mcp`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins inspect vision-mcp --json`; then loaded bundle MCP config with `node --import tsx` calling `loadEnabledBundleMcpConfig(...)` against the isolated home/config.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the temporary real OpenClaw setup:

```text
Proof root: /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins install --link /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp
Linked plugin path: /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp
Restart the gateway to load plugins.
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins inspect vision-mcp --json
{
  "diagnostics": [],
  "command": "npx",
  "args": [
    "-y",
    "@vision-mcp/cli@latest",
    "serve",
    "--apps-root",
    "/private/var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp/apps"
  ],
  "cwdEndsWith": true,
  "toolFilter": {
    "include": [
      "capsule.*",
      "vision_map.snapshot",
      "vision_map.list_apps",
      "vision_map.list_workflows",
      "vision_map.describe_workflow",
      "vision_map.describe",
      "vision_map.detect_state",
      "vision_map.list_actions",
      "vision_map.describe_action",
      "vision_map.perform_action",
      "vision_map.run_workflow",
      "vision_map.verify",
      "vision_map.repair_minimal",
      "vision_map.export_trace"
    ],
    "exclude": [
      "vision_map.click_at",
      "vision_map.type_text",
      "vision_map.press_key",
      "vision_map.scroll",
      "vision_map.init",
      "vision_map.apply_patch",
      "vision_map.add_control",
      "vision_map.commit_*",
      "vision_map.harvest_session"
    ]
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: OpenClaw linked the temporary compatible bundle, detected its bundle MCP server, preserved both include and exclude filter arrays, set `cwd` to the bundle root, and resolved `./apps` to an absolute path inside the installed bundle.

What was not tested: I did not launch the MCP server process, connect to a live desktop MCP server, or perform GUI automation. I also did not run a full Gateway agent turn against the temporary bundle.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: The proof uses a temporary local compatible bundle and the real OpenClaw plugin installer/loader, but the MCP server command itself is not started because this PR changes documentation and loader regression coverage, not MCP runtime execution behavior.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs -- src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts --reporter=verbose
pnpm format:check src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm check:docs
codex review --base origin/main
```

Regression coverage added: `src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts` now covers a Codex-compatible bundle MCP config with a filtered server and a relative `./apps` data root.

Validation results:

```text
src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts: 1 file passed, 8 tests passed
pnpm format:check src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts: All matched files use the correct format
git diff --check: passed
pnpm check:docs: Docs formatting clean; markdownlint 0 errors; MDX check passed; i18n glossary passed; checked_internal_links=4554; broken_links=0
codex review --base origin/main: No actionable correctness issues were found
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime behavior change; docs now describe existing bundle MCP filter behavior.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime change. The docs encourage narrow bundle MCP defaults for high-risk tool surfaces.

What is the highest-risk area? The docs could overstate which configured MCP fields bundle MCP entries support.

How is that risk mitigated? Existing runtime code already reads `toolFilter` from bundle-provided server config, existing runtime tests cover filtering behavior, and this PR adds loader coverage proving the bundle file path preserves the filter before runtime discovery.

## Current review state

AI-assisted: yes.

Next action: maintainer review.

target.title: fix(feishu): carry forward DM fallback and topic labels
target.body:
## Summary

Carry forward the remaining Feishu display-name behavior from #38958 after #51032 already landed the group-name session label fix.

This replacement should stay narrow:
- keep #51032's group-name implementation as the baseline
- add/repair DM display-name fallback through chatMembers with contact fallback
- preserve resolveSenderNames opt-out semantics
- scope sender/direct-member caches by account and relevant chat/sender identifiers
- surface topic/root labels only for topic-scoped Feishu sessions
- propagate permission guidance when DM fallback lookup hits a Feishu permission error

Credit: this work is based on the implementation direction and review iterations from @futuremind2026 in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/38958. The already-merged group-name baseline came from @jnuyao in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/51032.

## Validation

- pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot-sender-name.test.ts
- pnpm exec vitest run src/config/sessions.test.ts
- pnpm check:changed

ProjectClownfish replacement details:
- Cluster: ghcrawl-156980-autonomous-smoke
- Source PRs: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/38958
- Credit: Credit @futuremind2026 and source PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/38958 for the broader Feishu DM fallback, cache-scoping, permission guidance, and topic-label implementation direction.; Preserve @jnuyao and merged PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/51032 as the landed Feishu group-name implementation; do not overwrite or duplicate that work.; Mention in the PR body that this replacement carries forward the remaining behavior from #38958 because ProjectClownfish cannot safely update the original contributor branch.
- Validation: pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot-sender-name.test.ts; pnpm exec vitest run src/config/sessions.test.ts; pnpm check:changed

target.title: docs(memory): align descriptors and docs with recursive memory/**/*.md
target.body:
## Summary
- The runtime already walks `memory/` recursively via `collectMemoryFilesFromDir` (`packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`), so nested files like `memory/YYYY-MM/YYYY-MM-DD.md` are indexed and searched. But several agent-facing descriptors and public docs still said `memory/*.md`, which can mislead agents into thinking the index is flat.
- This PR updates the remaining stale references to `memory/**/*.md`. No runtime behavior change.

## Files touched
- `extensions/memory-core/index.ts` — lazy `memory_search` / `memory_get` descriptors
- `extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts` — concrete `memory_search` / `memory_get` descriptions
- `extensions/memory-core/src/prompt-section.ts` — system-prompt recall guidance
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` — `agents.defaults.memorySearch` help text
- `docs/concepts/memory-builtin.md`, `memory.md`, `system-prompt.md`
- `docs/gateway/config-agents.md`, `docs/reference/token-use.md`
- `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` regenerated via `pnpm config:docs:gen`

## Note
This supersedes #72748. That PR also carried QMD watcher changes that were independently resolved upstream by #81802 (`watch-settle.ts`), so the surviving value was just this wording alignment. Splitting it out as a small, behavior-neutral PR for easier review.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Agent-facing descriptors and public docs said `memory/*.md` even though the runtime already walks `memory/` recursively and indexes nested files such as `memory/YYYY-MM/YYYY-MM-DD.md`. This PR aligns the wording to `memory/**/*.md` so agents and readers see what is actually indexed. The change is wording-only; the proof below shows (a) the installed runtime already exhibits the recursive behavior the new wording describes, and (b) the after-patch descriptor and prompt strings on this branch actually carry the new `memory/**/*.md` wording when imported at runtime.

**Real environment tested**: Installed OpenClaw `2026.5.20` (npm global, current `latest` published build, ahead of the `acf265d` base this PR branches from), against the live workspace `~/.openclaw/workspace-moku` used by the gateway. Agent `main`, local embedding provider, per-agent SQLite memory store at `~/.openclaw/memory/main.sqlite`. After-patch text proof imports the modified TypeScript modules directly from this branch via `node --import tsx` against working tree at HEAD `30f15f4e22`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

Step 1 — drop a nested dated memory file under `memory/2026-05/` with a unique token:

```
cat > ~/.openclaw/workspace-moku/memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md <<'EOF'
Recursive memory search proof.
Unique token for PR 85899 smoke test: PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY
This file lives at memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md
EOF
```

Step 2 — reindex the live agent's memory store:

```
openclaw memory index
```

Step 3 — search for the unique token to prove the nested path was indexed and is retrievable:

```
openclaw memory search "PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY" --max-results 3
```

Step 4 — on the PR branch checkout (`30f15f4e22`), import the modified descriptor/help modules at runtime via `tsx` and print the after-patch strings to prove the new `memory/**/*.md` wording is observable from a real runtime call site (not just the diff):

```
node --import tsx _proof-c.mjs   # imports FIELD_HELP and buildPromptSection from this branch
```

**After-fix evidence**: Terminal output from the installed `openclaw 2026.5.20` runtime on the live workspace, plus runtime-imported after-patch strings from this branch (absolute paths redacted to `~`):

```
$ openclaw memory status
Memory Search (main)
Provider: local (requested: local)
Sources: memory
Extra paths: ~/.openclaw/shared-memory
Indexed: 105/105 files . 933 chunks
Workspace: ~/.openclaw/workspace-moku
By source:
  memory . 105/105 files . 933 chunks
```

```
$ openclaw memory index
[node-llama-cpp] ... falling back to using no GPU
Memory index updated (main).
```

```
$ openclaw memory search "PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY" --max-results 3
[node-llama-cpp] ... falling back to using no GPU
0.812 memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md:1-9
    Recursive memory search proof.

**Observed result after fix**: (a) The freshly written nested file `memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md` (two levels deep under `memory/`) was indexed by `openclaw memory index` and returned as the top match (score `0.812`) for the unique token. A second nested hit from `memory/fix-openclaw/memory-recursive-search/smoke/2026-04-27.md` (three levels deep) confirms recursion is not limited to one level. (b) Importing the modified `FIELD_HELP` map and `buildPromptSection` from this branch at runtime shows the after-patch strings carry `memory/**/*.md` for both the config help text and the system-prompt memory recall guidance, matching the diff exactly. target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.
